{{Infobox organization | name = Shinshū Takada-ha | native_name = 真宗高田派 | image = [[File:Shinshu Takada-ha Shumuin.JPG|250px]] | caption = The Religious Affairs Office at the head temple [[Senjuji]] | type = [[Religious corporation]] | headquarters = 2819 Isshinden-chō, Tsu City, Mie Prefecture | leader_title = Hōshu (Head Priest) | leader_name = Jishō | website = {{URL|http://www.senjuji.or.jp/}} }}
'''Shinshū Takada-ha''' (真宗高田派) is a branch of {{ill|Jōdo Shinshū|ja|淡土真宗}}, with its head temple at {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺}} in {{ill|Tsu, Mie|ja|渡哥市}}. It traces its origins to the disciples {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏}} and {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智}}, who led the Highada-monto community in the medieval period. The sect has approximately 640 affiliated temples.
== History ==
=== Founding Period === In {{ill|1205|ja|1205年}}, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇}}, the practice of {{ill|Senju Nembutsu|ja|専修念仏}}, propagated by {{ill|Hōnen|ja|法然}} and his disciples, was banned, leading to the exile of {{ill|Shinran|ja|親熊}} to {{ill|Echigo Province|ja|越後国}}. After being pardoned in {{ill|1211|ja|1211年}}, Shinran traveled to {{ill|Zenkoji|ja|善光寺}} and then settled in {{ill|Hitachi Province|ja|常陽国}}, where he began his missionary activities.
Shinran established his base at {{ill|Nishi Nembutsuji|ja|西念仏寺}} in Hitachi, and in {{ill|1224|ja|1224年}}, he completed his seminal work, {{ill|Kyōgyōshinshō|ja|顯淡土真実教行証文類}} ('Teaching, Practice, Faith, and Attainment'), which laid the doctrinal foundation for Jōdo Shinshū.
In {{ill|1225|ja|1225年}}, according to tradition, Shinran received a revelation instructing him to establish a temple in Highada. With the support of {{ill|Ōuchi Clan|ja|大内氏}}, the ruler of {{ill|Mooka Castle|ja|真岡城}}, he enshrined a replica of the {{ill|Zenkoji-style Amida Triad|ja|善光寺式阿弥陀三宗}} in what became {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺}}, marking the origins of the Highada-monto tradition and the Shinshū Takada-ha sect.
The temple was officially named ''Senshū Amida-ji'' in {{ill|1226|ja|1226年}} by imperial decree, and Shinran spent about seven years there before returning to Kyoto. After Shinran's departure, his prominent disciple {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏}} succeeded him as the second head priest of Senjuji, followed by {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智}}.
=== Sengoku Period === During the 15th century, the sect expanded significantly under the leadership of the 10th head priest, {{ill|Shin'e|ja|真恵}}, who actively spread Jōdo Shinshū teachings across {{ill|Tōkai region|ja|東海地方}} and {{ill|Hokuriku region|ja|北陸地方}}. However, conflicts arose with {{ill|Rennyo|ja|蓮妃}}, the influential head of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺}}, leading many followers and temples to shift allegiance to the Honganji faction. In {{ill|1465|ja|1465年}}, Shin'e petitioned {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比士山}} to recognize Takada-ha as separate from Honganji, which was granted.
Despite setbacks due to military conflicts, including the {{ill|Echizen Ikkō-Ikki|ja|越前一向一挑}}, Takada-ha retained a strong presence in certain regions. By the 16th century, the sect moved its primary temple from {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}} to {{ill|Isshinden|ja|一身田}} in {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国}}, where it remains today.
=== Edo Period === During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Takada-ha maintained its status as one of the largest Jōdo Shinshū sects after the split of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺}} into the {{ill|Nishi Honganji|ja|西本願寺}} and {{ill|Higashi Honganji|ja|東本願寺}} branches. The sect continued to flourish, with its head temple, Senjuji, recognized as a ''jun-monzeki'' (quasi-imperial temple).
== Major Temples == ; Head Temple * {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺}} (Mooka, Tochigi Prefecture)
; Main Temple * {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺}} (Isshinden, Tsu, Mie Prefecture)
; Branch Temples * Kyoto Betsuin * {{ill|Nagoya Betsuin|ja|專修寺名古屋別院}} * Kobe Betsuin * Hokkaido Betsuin (Kiyota-ku, Sapporo, Hokkaido) * Fukui Betsuin * Kanto Betsuin * Yokohama Betsuin
== Characteristics == Takada-ha uniquely uses ''Bunrui-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of the Pure Land|ja|文類慰}}) for evening services, unlike other Jōdo Shinshū sects that primarily recite ''Shōshin-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of True Faith|ja|正信慰}}). Additionally, its temples traditionally do not use {{ill|Tannishō|ja|商異止}}, as the sect preserves a large collection of Shinran's original writings.
== External Links == * [http://www.honji-senjuji.jp/ Honji Senjuji] * [http://www.senjuji.or.jp/ Senjuji]

{{Infobox organization | name = Shinshū Takada-ha | native_name = 真宗高田派 | image = [[File:Shinshu Takada-ha Shumuin.JPG|250px]] | caption = The Religious Affairs Office at the head temple [[Senjuji]] | type = [[Religious corporation]] | headquarters = 2819 Isshinden-chō, Tsu City, Mie Prefecture | leader_title = Hōshu (Head Priest) | leader_name = Jishō | website = {{URL|http://www.senjuji.or.jp/}} }}
'''Shinshū Takada-ha''' (真宗高田派) is a branch of {{ill|Jōdo Shinshū|ja|淡土真宗}}, with its head temple at {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺}} in {{ill|Tsu, Mie|ja|渡哥市}}. It traces its origins to the disciples {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏}} and {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智}}, who led the Highada-monto community in the medieval period. The sect has approximately 640 affiliated temples.
== History ==
=== Founding Period === In {{ill|1205|ja|1205年}}, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇}}, the practice of {{ill|Senju Nembutsu|ja|専修念仏}}, propagated by {{ill|Hōnen|ja|法然}} and his disciples, was banned, leading to the exile of {{ill|Shinran|ja|親熊}} to {{ill|Echigo Province|ja|越後国}}. After being pardoned in {{ill|1211|ja|1211年}}, Shinran traveled to {{ill|Zenkoji|ja|善光寺}} and then settled in {{ill|Hitachi Province|ja|常陽国}}, where he began his missionary activities.
Shinran established his base at {{ill|Nishi Nembutsuji|ja|西念仏寺}} in Hitachi, and in {{ill|1224|ja|1224年}}, he completed his seminal work, {{ill|Kyōgyōshinshō|ja|顯淡土真実教行証文類}} ('Teaching, Practice, Faith, and Attainment'), which laid the doctrinal foundation for Jōdo Shinshū.
In {{ill|1225|ja|1225年}}, according to tradition, Shinran received a revelation instructing him to establish a temple in Highada. With the support of {{ill|Ōuchi Clan|ja|大内氏}}, the ruler of {{ill|Mooka Castle|ja|真岡城}}, he enshrined a replica of the {{ill|Zenkoji-style Amida Triad|ja|善光寺式阿弥陀三宗}} in what became {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺}}, marking the origins of the Highada-monto tradition and the Shinshū Takada-ha sect.
The temple was officially named ''Senshū Amida-ji'' in {{ill|1226|ja|1226年}} by imperial decree, and Shinran spent about seven years there before returning to Kyoto. After Shinran's departure, his prominent disciple {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏}} succeeded him as the second head priest of Senjuji, followed by {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智}}.
=== Sengoku Period === During the 15th century, the sect expanded significantly under the leadership of the 10th head priest, {{ill|Shin'e|ja|真恵}}, who actively spread Jōdo Shinshū teachings across {{ill|Tōkai region|ja|東海地方}} and {{ill|Hokuriku region|ja|北陸地方}}. However, conflicts arose with {{ill|Rennyo|ja|蓮妃}}, the influential head of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺}}, leading many followers and temples to shift allegiance to the Honganji faction. In {{ill|1465|ja|1465年}}, Shin'e petitioned {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比士山}} to recognize Takada-ha as separate from Honganji, which was granted.
Despite setbacks due to military conflicts, including the {{ill|Echizen Ikkō-Ikki|ja|越前一向一挑}}, Takada-ha retained a strong presence in certain regions. By the 16th century, the sect moved its primary temple from {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}} to {{ill|Isshinden|ja|一身田}} in {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国}}, where it remains today.
=== Edo Period === During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Takada-ha maintained its status as one of the largest Jōdo Shinshū sects after the split of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺}} into the {{ill|Nishi Honganji|ja|西本願寺}} and {{ill|Higashi Honganji|ja|東本願寺}} branches. The sect continued to flourish, with its head temple, Senjuji, recognized as a ''jun-monzeki'' (quasi-imperial temple).
== Major Temples == ; Head Temple * {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺}} (Mooka, Tochigi Prefecture)
; Main Temple * {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺}} (Isshinden, Tsu, Mie Prefecture)
; Branch Temples * Kyoto Betsuin * {{ill|Nagoya Betsuin|ja|專修寺名古屋別院}} * Kobe Betsuin * Hokkaido Betsuin (Kiyota-ku, Sapporo, Hokkaido) * Fukui Betsuin * Kanto Betsuin * Yokohama Betsuin
== Characteristics == Takada-ha uniquely uses ''Bunrui-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of the Pure Land|ja|文類慰}}) for evening services, unlike other Jōdo Shinshū sects that primarily recite ''Shōshin-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of True Faith|ja|正信慰}}). Additionally, its temples traditionally do not use {{ill|Tannishō|ja|商異止}}, as the sect preserves a large collection of Shinran's original writings.
== External Links == * [http://www.honji-senjuji.jp/ Honji Senjuji] * [http://www.senjuji.or.jp/ Senjuji]

With the support of {{ill|Ōuchi Clan|ja|大内氏}}, the ruler of {{ill|Mooka Castle|ja|真岡城}}, he enshrined a replica of the {{ill|Zenkoji-style Amida Triad|ja|善光寺式阿弥陀三宗}} in what became {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺}}, marking the origins of the Highada-monto tradition and the Shinshū Takada-ha sect.
The temple was officially named ''Senshū Amida-ji'' in {{ill|1226|ja|1226年}} by imperial decree, and Shinran spent about seven years there before returning to Kyoto. After Shinran's departure, his prominent disciple {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏}} succeeded him as the second head priest of Senjuji, followed by {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智}}.
=== Sengoku Period === During the 15th century, the sect expanded significantly under the leadership of the 10th head priest, {{ill|Shin'e|ja|真恵}}, who actively spread Jōdo Shinshū teachings across {{ill|Tōkai region|ja|東海地方}} and {{ill|Hokuriku region|ja|北陸地方}}. However, conflicts arose with {{ill|Rennyo|ja|蓮妃}}, the influential head of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺}}, leading many followers and temples to shift allegiance to the Honganji faction. In {{ill|1465|ja|1465年}}, Shin'e petitioned {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比士山}} to recognize Takada-ha as separate from Honganji, which was granted.
Despite setbacks due to military conflicts, including the {{ill|Echizen Ikkō-Ikki|ja|越前一向一挑}}, Takada-ha retained a strong presence in certain regions. By the 16th century, the sect moved its primary temple from {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}} to {{ill|Isshinden|ja|一身田}} in {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国}}, where it remains today.
=== Edo Period === During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Takada-ha maintained its status as one of the largest Jōdo Shinshū sects after the split of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺}} into the {{ill|Nishi Honganji|ja|西本願寺}} and {{ill|Higashi Honganji|ja|東本願寺}} branches. The sect continued to flourish, with its head temple, Senjuji, recognized as a ''jun-monzeki'' (quasi-imperial temple).
== Major Temples == ; Head Temple * {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺}} (Mooka, Tochigi Prefecture)
; Main Temple * {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺}} (Isshinden, Tsu, Mie Prefecture)
; Branch Temples * Kyoto Betsuin * {{ill|Nagoya Betsuin|ja|專修寺名古屋別院}} * Kobe Betsuin * Hokkaido Betsuin (Kiyota-ku, Sapporo, Hokkaido) * Fukui Betsuin * Kanto Betsuin * Yokohama Betsuin
== Characteristics == Takada-ha uniquely uses ''Bunrui-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of the Pure Land|ja|文類慰}}) for evening services, unlike other Jōdo Shinshū sects that primarily recite ''Shōshin-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of True Faith|ja|正信慰}}). Additionally, its temples traditionally do not use {{ill|Tannishō|ja|商異止}}, as the sect preserves a large collection of Shinran's original writings.
== External Links == * [http://www.honji-senjuji.jp/ Honji Senjuji] * [http://www.senjuji.or.jp/ Senjuji]

'''Shinshū Takada-ha''' (真宗高田派) is a branch of {{ill|Jōdo Shinshū|ja|淡土真宗}}, with its head temple at {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺}} in {{ill|Tsu, Mie|ja|渡哥市}}. It traces its origins to the disciples {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏}} and {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智}}, who led the Highada-monto community in the medieval period. The sect has approximately 640 affiliated temples.
== History ==
=== Founding Period === In {{ill|1205|ja|1205年}}, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇}}, the practice of {{ill|Senju Nembutsu|ja|専修念仏}}, propagated by {{ill|Hōnen|ja|法然}} and his disciples, was banned, leading to the exile of {{ill|Shinran|ja|親熊}} to {{ill|Echigo Province|ja|越後国}}. After being pardoned in {{ill|1211|ja|1211年}}, Shinran traveled to {{ill|Zenkoji|ja|善光寺}} and then settled in {{ill|Hitachi Province|ja|常陽国}}, where he began his missionary activities.
Shinran established his base at {{ill|Nishi Nembutsuji|ja|西念仏寺}} in Hitachi, and in {{ill|1224|ja|1224年}}, he completed his seminal work, {{ill|Kyōgyōshinshō|ja|顯淡土真実教行証文類}} ('Teaching, Practice, Faith, and Attainment'), which laid the doctrinal foundation for Jōdo Shinshū.
In {{ill|1225|ja|1225年}}, according to tradition, Shinran received a revelation instructing him to establish a temple in Highada. With the support of {{ill|Ōuchi Clan|ja|大内氏}}, the ruler of {{ill|Mooka Castle|ja|真岡城}}, he enshrined a replica of the {{ill|Zenkoji-style Amida Triad|ja|善光寺式阿弥陀三宗}} in what became {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺}}, marking the origins of the Highada-monto tradition and the Shinshū Takada-ha sect.
The temple was officially named ''Senshū Amida-ji'' in {{ill|1226|ja|1226年}} by imperial decree, and Shinran spent about seven years there before returning to Kyoto. After Shinran's departure, his prominent disciple {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏}} succeeded him as the second head priest of Senjuji, followed by {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智}}.
=== Sengoku Period === During the 15th century, the sect expanded significantly under the leadership of the 10th head priest, {{ill|Shin'e|ja|真恵}}, who actively spread Jōdo Shinshū teachings across {{ill|Tōkai region|ja|東海地方}} and {{ill|Hokuriku region|ja|北陸地方}}. However, conflicts arose with {{ill|Rennyo|ja|蓮妃}}, the influential head of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺}}, leading many followers and temples to shift allegiance to the Honganji faction. In {{ill|1465|ja|1465年}}, Shin'e petitioned {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比士山}} to recognize Takada-ha as separate from Honganji, which was granted.
Despite setbacks due to military conflicts, including the {{ill|Echizen Ikkō-Ikki|ja|越前一向一挑}}, Takada-ha retained a strong presence in certain regions. By the 16th century, the sect moved its primary temple from {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}} to {{ill|Isshinden|ja|一身田}} in {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国}}, where it remains today.
=== Edo Period === During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Takada-ha maintained its status as one of the largest Jōdo Shinshū sects after the split of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺}} into the {{ill|Nishi Honganji|ja|西本願寺}} and {{ill|Higashi Honganji|ja|東本願寺}} branches. The sect continued to flourish, with its head temple, Senjuji, recognized as a ''jun-monzeki'' (quasi-imperial temple).
== Major Temples == ; Head Temple * {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺}} (Mooka, Tochigi Prefecture)
; Main Temple * {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺}} (Isshinden, Tsu, Mie Prefecture)
; Branch Temples * Kyoto Betsuin * {{ill|Nagoya Betsuin|ja|專修寺名古屋別院}} * Kobe Betsuin * Hokkaido Betsuin (Kiyota-ku, Sapporo, Hokkaido) * Fukui Betsuin * Kanto Betsuin * Yokohama Betsuin
== Characteristics == Takada-ha uniquely uses ''Bunrui-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of the Pure Land|ja|文類慰}}) for evening services, unlike other Jōdo Shinshū sects that primarily recite ''Shōshin-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of True Faith|ja|正信慰}}). Additionally, its temples traditionally do not use {{ill|Tannishō|ja|商異止}}, as the sect preserves a large collection of Shinran's original writings.

revision.text:
{{Infobox organization | name = Shinshū Takada-ha | native_name = 真宗高田派 | image = [[File:Shinshu Takada-ha Shumuin.JPG|250px]] | caption = The Religious Affairs Office at the head temple [[Senjuji]] | type = [[Religious corporation]] | headquarters = 2819 Isshinden-chō, Tsu City, Mie Prefecture | leader_title = Hōshu (Head Priest) | leader_name = Jishō | website = {{URL|http://www.senjuji.or.jp/}} }}
'''Shinshū Takada-ha''' (真宗高田派) is a branch of {{ill|Jōdo Shinshū|ja|淡土真宗}}, with its head temple at {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺}} in {{ill|Tsu, Mie|ja|渡哥市}}. It traces its origins to the disciples {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏}} and {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智}}, who led the Highada-monto community in the medieval period. The sect has approximately 640 affiliated temples.
== History ==
=== Founding Period === In {{ill|1205|ja|1205年}}, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇}}, the practice of {{ill|Senju Nembutsu|ja|専修念仏}}, propagated by {{ill|Hōnen|ja|法然}} and his disciples, was banned, leading to the exile of {{ill|Shinran|ja|親熊}} to {{ill|Echigo Province|ja|越後国}}. After being pardoned in {{ill|1211|ja|1211年}}, Shinran traveled to {{ill|Zenkoji|ja|善光寺}} and then settled in {{ill|Hitachi Province|ja|常陽国}}, where he began his missionary activities.
Shinran established his base at {{ill|Nishi Nembutsuji|ja|西念仏寺}} in Hitachi, and in {{ill|1224|ja|1224年}}, he completed his seminal work, {{ill|Kyōgyōshinshō|ja|顯淡土真実教行証文類}} ('Teaching, Practice, Faith, and Attainment'), which laid the doctrinal foundation for Jōdo Shinshū.
In {{ill|1225|ja|1225年}}, according to tradition, Shinran received a revelation instructing him to establish a temple in Highada. With the support of {{ill|Ōuchi Clan|ja|大内氏}}, the ruler of {{ill|Mooka Castle|ja|真岡城}}, he enshrined a replica of the {{ill|Zenkoji-style Amida Triad|ja|善光寺式阿弥陀三宗}} in what became {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺}}, marking the origins of the Highada-monto tradition and the Shinshū Takada-ha sect.
The temple was officially named ''Senshū Amida-ji'' in {{ill|1226|ja|1226年}} by imperial decree, and Shinran spent about seven years there before returning to Kyoto. After Shinran's departure, his prominent disciple {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏}} succeeded him as the second head priest of Senjuji, followed by {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智}}.
=== Sengoku Period === During the 15th century, the sect expanded significantly under the leadership of the 10th head priest, {{ill|Shin'e|ja|真恵}}, who actively spread Jōdo Shinshū teachings across {{ill|Tōkai region|ja|東海地方}} and {{ill|Hokuriku region|ja|北陸地方}}. However, conflicts arose with {{ill|Rennyo|ja|蓮妃}}, the influential head of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺}}, leading many followers and temples to shift allegiance to the Honganji faction. In {{ill|1465|ja|1465年}}, Shin'e petitioned {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比士山}} to recognize Takada-ha as separate from Honganji, which was granted.
Despite setbacks due to military conflicts, including the {{ill|Echizen Ikkō-Ikki|ja|越前一向一挑}}, Takada-ha retained a strong presence in certain regions. By the 16th century, the sect moved its primary temple from {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}} to {{ill|Isshinden|ja|一身田}} in {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国}}, where it remains today.
=== Edo Period === During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Takada-ha maintained its status as one of the largest Jōdo Shinshū sects after the split of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺}} into the {{ill|Nishi Honganji|ja|西本願寺}} and {{ill|Higashi Honganji|ja|東本願寺}} branches. The sect continued to flourish, with its head temple, Senjuji, recognized as a ''jun-monzeki'' (quasi-imperial temple).
== Major Temples == ; Head Temple * {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺}} (Mooka, Tochigi Prefecture)
; Main Temple * {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺}} (Isshinden, Tsu, Mie Prefecture)
; Branch Temples * Kyoto Betsuin * {{ill|Nagoya Betsuin|ja|專修寺名古屋別院}} * Kobe Betsuin * Hokkaido Betsuin (Kiyota-ku, Sapporo, Hokkaido) * Fukui Betsuin * Kanto Betsuin * Yokohama Betsuin
== Characteristics == Takada-ha uniquely uses ''Bunrui-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of the Pure Land|ja|文類慰}}) for evening services, unlike other Jōdo Shinshū sects that primarily recite ''Shōshin-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of True Faith|ja|正信慰}}). Additionally, its temples traditionally do not use {{ill|Tannishō|ja|商異止}}, as the sect preserves a large collection of Shinran's original writings.
== External Links == * [http://www.honji-senjuji.jp/ Honji Senjuji] * [http://www.senjuji.or.jp/ Senjuji]

== References == <references />
== Bibliography == * {{ill|Kuroda Toshio|en}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha-->[[ja:黒田俊雄]]. *Temple and Shrine Powers: Another Medieval Society.* Iwanami Shinsho, 1980. * {{ill|Itō Masatoshi|en}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha-->[[ja:伊藤正敏]]. *The Medieval Religious Powers: Migration and Connection.* Chikuma Shinsho, 2008.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kenmitsu System|ja|顕密体制|en}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha-->[[ja:顕密体制]] * {{ill|Kenmon System Theory|ja|権門体制|en}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha-->[[ja:権門体制]] * {{ill|Right of asylum|ja|アジール権|fr|Droit d'asile|en}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha-->[[ja:アジール]] * {{ill|Temple Precinct Cities|ja|境内都市|en}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha-->[[ja:境内都市]] * {{ill|Jinaimachi|fr|Jinaicho|ja|寺内町|lt=Teramachi|en}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha-->[[ja:寺内町]] * {{ill|Sōhei|en}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha-->[[ja:僧兵]] * {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha-->[[ja:神人]]

{{Infobox organization | name = Shinshū Takada-ha | native_name = 真宗高田派 | image = [[File:Shinshu Takada-ha Shumuin.JPG|250px]] | caption = The Religious Affairs Office at the head temple [[Senjuji]] | type = [[Religious corporation]] | headquarters = 2819 Isshinden-chō, Tsu City, Mie Prefecture | leader_title = Hōshu (Head Priest) | leader_name = Jishō | website = {{URL|http://www.senjuji.or.jp/}} }}
'''Shinshū Takada-ha''' (真宗高田派) is a branch of {{ill|Jōdo Shinshū|ja|淡土真宗}}, with its head temple at {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺}} in {{ill|Tsu, Mie|ja|渡哥市}}. It traces its origins to the disciples {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏}} and {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智}}, who led the Highada-monto community in the medieval period. The sect has approximately 640 affiliated temples.
== History ==
=== Founding Period === In {{ill|1205|ja|1205年}}, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇}}, the practice of {{ill|Senju Nembutsu|ja|専修念仏}}, propagated by {{ill|Hōnen|ja|法然}} and his disciples, was banned, leading to the exile of {{ill|Shinran|ja|親熊}} to {{ill|Echigo Province|ja|越後国}}. After being pardoned in {{ill|1211|ja|1211年}}, Shinran traveled to {{ill|Zenkoji|ja|善光寺}} and then settled in {{ill|Hitachi Province|ja|常陽国}}, where he began his missionary activities.
Shinran established his base at {{ill|Nishi Nembutsuji|ja|西念仏寺}} in Hitachi, and in {{ill|1224|ja|1224年}}, he completed his seminal work, {{ill|Kyōgyōshinshō|ja|顯淡土真実教行証文類}} ('Teaching, Practice, Faith, and Attainment'), which laid the doctrinal foundation for Jōdo Shinshū.
In {{ill|1225|ja|1225年}}, according to tradition, Shinran received a revelation instructing him to establish a temple in Highada. With the support of {{ill|Ōuchi Clan|ja|大内氏}}, the ruler of {{ill|Mooka Castle|ja|真岡城}}, he enshrined a replica of the {{ill|Zenkoji-style Amida Triad|ja|善光寺式阿弥陀三宗}} in what became {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺}}, marking the origins of the Highada-monto tradition and the Shinshū Takada-ha sect.
The temple was officially named ''Senshū Amida-ji'' in {{ill|1226|ja|1226年}} by imperial decree, and Shinran spent about seven years there before returning to Kyoto. After Shinran's departure, his prominent disciple {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏}} succeeded him as the second head priest of Senjuji, followed by {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智}}.
=== Sengoku Period === During the 15th century, the sect expanded significantly under the leadership of the 10th head priest, {{ill|Shin'e|ja|真恵}}, who actively spread Jōdo Shinshū teachings across {{ill|Tōkai region|ja|東海地方}} and {{ill|Hokuriku region|ja|北陸地方}}. However, conflicts arose with {{ill|Rennyo|ja|蓮妃}}, the influential head of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺}}, leading many followers and temples to shift allegiance to the Honganji faction. In {{ill|1465|ja|1465年}}, Shin'e petitioned {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比士山}} to recognize Takada-ha as separate from Honganji, which was granted.
Despite setbacks due to military conflicts, including the {{ill|Echizen Ikkō-Ikki|ja|越前一向一挑}}, Takada-ha retained a strong presence in certain regions. By the 16th century, the sect moved its primary temple from {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}} to {{ill|Isshinden|ja|一身田}} in {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国}}, where it remains today.
=== Edo Period === During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Takada-ha maintained its status as one of the largest Jōdo Shinshū sects after the split of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺}} into the {{ill|Nishi Honganji|ja|西本願寺}} and {{ill|Higashi Honganji|ja|東本願寺}} branches. The sect continued to flourish, with its head temple, Senjuji, recognized as a ''jun-monzeki'' (quasi-imperial temple).
== Major Temples == ; Head Temple * {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺}} (Mooka, Tochigi Prefecture)
; Main Temple * {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺}} (Isshinden, Tsu, Mie Prefecture)
; Branch Temples * Kyoto Betsuin * {{ill|Nagoya Betsuin|ja|專修寺名古屋別院}} * Kobe Betsuin * Hokkaido Betsuin (Kiyota-ku, Sapporo, Hokkaido) * Fukui Betsuin * Kanto Betsuin * Yokohama Betsuin
== Characteristics == Takada-ha uniquely uses ''Bunrui-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of the Pure Land|ja|文類慰}}) for evening services, unlike other Jōdo Shinshū sects that primarily recite ''Shōshin-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of True Faith|ja|正信慰}}). Additionally, its temples traditionally do not use {{ill|Tannishō|ja|商異止}}, as the sect preserves a large collection of Shinran's original writings.
== External Links == * [http://www.honji-senjuji.jp/ Honji Senjuji] * [http://www.senjuji.or.jp/ Senjuji]
[[Category:Jōdo Shinshū sects]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist sects]] [[Category:Religious organizations in Japan]] [[ja:真宗高田派]]

== References == <references />
== Bibliography == * {{ill|Kuroda Toshio|en}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha-->[[ja:黒田俊雄]]. *Temple and Shrine Powers: Another Medieval Society.* Iwanami Shinsho, 1980. * {{ill|Itō Masatoshi|en}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha-->[[ja:伊藤正敏]]. *The Medieval Religious Powers: Migration and Connection.* Chikuma Shinsho, 2008.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kenmitsu System|ja|顕密体制|en}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha-->[[ja:顕密体制]] * {{ill|Kenmon System Theory|ja|権門体制|en}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha-->[[ja:権門体制]] * {{ill|Right of asylum|ja|アジール権|fr|Droit d'asile|en}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha-->[[ja:アジール]] * {{ill|Temple Precinct Cities|ja|境内都市|en}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha-->[[ja:境内都市]] * {{ill|Jinaimachi|fr|Jinaicho|ja|寺内町|lt=Teramachi|en}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha-->[[ja:寺内町]] * {{ill|Sōhei|en}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha-->[[ja:僧兵]] * {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha-->[[ja:神人]]

In {{ill|1225|ja|1225年}}, according to tradition, Shinran received a revelation instructing him to establish a temple in Highada. With the support of {{ill|Ōuchi Clan|ja|大内氏}}, the ruler of {{ill|Mooka Castle|ja|真岡城}}, he enshrined a replica of the {{ill|Zenkoji-style Amida Triad|ja|善光寺式阿弥陀三宗}} in what became {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺}}, marking the origins of the Highada-monto tradition and the Shinshū Takada-ha sect.
The temple was officially named ''Senshū Amida-ji'' in {{ill|1226|ja|1226年}} by imperial decree, and Shinran spent about seven years there before returning to Kyoto. After Shinran's departure, his prominent disciple {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏}} succeeded him as the second head priest of Senjuji, followed by {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智}}.
=== Sengoku Period === During the 15th century, the sect expanded significantly under the leadership of the 10th head priest, {{ill|Shin'e|ja|真恵}}, who actively spread Jōdo Shinshū teachings across {{ill|Tōkai region|ja|東海地方}} and {{ill|Hokuriku region|ja|北陸地方}}. However, conflicts arose with {{ill|Rennyo|ja|蓮妃}}, the influential head of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺}}, leading many followers and temples to shift allegiance to the Honganji faction. In {{ill|1465|ja|1465年}}, Shin'e petitioned {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比士山}} to recognize Takada-ha as separate from Honganji, which was granted.
Despite setbacks due to military conflicts, including the {{ill|Echizen Ikkō-Ikki|ja|越前一向一挑}}, Takada-ha retained a strong presence in certain regions. By the 16th century, the sect moved its primary temple from {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}} to {{ill|Isshinden|ja|一身田}} in {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国}}, where it remains today.
=== Edo Period === During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Takada-ha maintained its status as one of the largest Jōdo Shinshū sects after the split of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺}} into the {{ill|Nishi Honganji|ja|西本願寺}} and {{ill|Higashi Honganji|ja|東本願寺}} branches. The sect continued to flourish, with its head temple, Senjuji, recognized as a ''jun-monzeki'' (quasi-imperial temple).
== Major Temples == ; Head Temple * {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺}} (Mooka, Tochigi Prefecture)
; Main Temple * {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺}} (Isshinden, Tsu, Mie Prefecture)
; Branch Temples * Kyoto Betsuin * {{ill|Nagoya Betsuin|ja|專修寺名古屋別院}} * Kobe Betsuin * Hokkaido Betsuin (Kiyota-ku, Sapporo, Hokkaido) * Fukui Betsuin * Kanto Betsuin * Yokohama Betsuin
== Characteristics == Takada-ha uniquely uses ''Bunrui-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of the Pure Land|ja|文類慰}}) for evening services, unlike other Jōdo Shinshū sects that primarily recite ''Shōshin-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of True Faith|ja|正信慰}}). Additionally, its temples traditionally do not use {{ill|Tannishō|ja|商異止}}, as the sect preserves a large collection of Shinran's original writings.
== External Links == * [http://www.honji-senjuji.jp/ Honji Senjuji] * [http://www.senjuji.or.jp/ Senjuji]

{{Infobox organization | name = Shinshū Takada-ha | native_name = 真宗高田派 | image = [[File:Shinshu Takada-ha Shumuin.JPG|250px]] | caption = The Religious Affairs Office at the head temple [[Senjuji]] | type = [[Religious corporation]] | headquarters = 2819 Isshinden-chō, Tsu City, Mie Prefecture | leader_title = Hōshu (Head Priest) | leader_name = Jishō | website = {{URL|http://www.senjuji.or.jp/}} }}
'''Shinshū Takada-ha''' (真宗高田派) is a branch of {{ill|Jōdo Shinshū|ja|淡土真宗}}, with its head temple at {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺}} in {{ill|Tsu, Mie|ja|渡哥市}}. It traces its origins to the disciples {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏}} and {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智}}, who led the Highada-monto community in the medieval period. The sect has approximately 640 affiliated temples.
== History ==
=== Founding Period === In {{ill|1205|ja|1205年}}, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇}}, the practice of {{ill|Senju Nembutsu|ja|専修念仏}}, propagated by {{ill|Hōnen|ja|法然}} and his disciples, was banned, leading to the exile of {{ill|Shinran|ja|親熊}} to {{ill|Echigo Province|ja|越後国}}. After being pardoned in {{ill|1211|ja|1211年}}, Shinran traveled to {{ill|Zenkoji|ja|善光寺}} and then settled in {{ill|Hitachi Province|ja|常陽国}}, where he began his missionary activities.
Shinran established his base at {{ill|Nishi Nembutsuji|ja|西念仏寺}} in Hitachi, and in {{ill|1224|ja|1224年}}, he completed his seminal work, {{ill|Kyōgyōshinshō|ja|顯淡土真実教行証文類}} ('Teaching, Practice, Faith, and Attainment'), which laid the doctrinal foundation for Jōdo Shinshū.
In {{ill|1225|ja|1225年}}, according to tradition, Shinran received a revelation instructing him to establish a temple in Highada. With the support of {{ill|Ōuchi Clan|ja|大内氏}}, the ruler of {{ill|Mooka Castle|ja|真岡城}}, he enshrined a replica of the {{ill|Zenkoji-style Amida Triad|ja|善光寺式阿弥陀三宗}} in what became {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺}}, marking the origins of the Highada-monto tradition and the Shinshū Takada-ha sect.
The temple was officially named ''Senshū Amida-ji'' in {{ill|1226|ja|1226年}} by imperial decree, and Shinran spent about seven years there before returning to Kyoto. After Shinran's departure, his prominent disciple {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏}} succeeded him as the second head priest of Senjuji, followed by {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智}}.
=== Sengoku Period === During the 15th century, the sect expanded significantly under the leadership of the 10th head priest, {{ill|Shin'e|ja|真恵}}, who actively spread Jōdo Shinshū teachings across {{ill|Tōkai region|ja|東海地方}} and {{ill|Hokuriku region|ja|北陸地方}}. However, conflicts arose with {{ill|Rennyo|ja|蓮妃}}, the influential head of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺}}, leading many followers and temples to shift allegiance to the Honganji faction. In {{ill|1465|ja|1465年}}, Shin'e petitioned {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比士山}} to recognize Takada-ha as separate from Honganji, which was granted.
Despite setbacks due to military conflicts, including the {{ill|Echizen Ikkō-Ikki|ja|越前一向一挑}}, Takada-ha retained a strong presence in certain regions. By the 16th century, the sect moved its primary temple from {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}} to {{ill|Isshinden|ja|一身田}} in {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国}}, where it remains today.
=== Edo Period === During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Takada-ha maintained its status as one of the largest Jōdo Shinshū sects after the split of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺}} into the {{ill|Nishi Honganji|ja|西本願寺}} and {{ill|Higashi Honganji|ja|東本願寺}} branches. The sect continued to flourish, with its head temple, Senjuji, recognized as a ''jun-monzeki'' (quasi-imperial temple).
== Major Temples == ; Head Temple * {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺}} (Mooka, Tochigi Prefecture)
; Main Temple * {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺}} (Isshinden, Tsu, Mie Prefecture)
; Branch Temples * Kyoto Betsuin * {{ill|Nagoya Betsuin|ja|專修寺名古屋別院}} * Kobe Betsuin * Hokkaido Betsuin (Kiyota-ku, Sapporo, Hokkaido) * Fukui Betsuin * Kanto Betsuin * Yokohama Betsuin
== Characteristics == Takada-ha uniquely uses ''Bunrui-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of the Pure Land|ja|文類慰}}) for evening services, unlike other Jōdo Shinshū sects that primarily recite ''Shōshin-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of True Faith|ja|正信慰}}). Additionally, its temples traditionally do not use {{ill|Tannishō|ja|商異止}}, as the sect preserves a large collection of Shinran's original writings.
== External Links == * [http://www.honji-senjuji.jp/ Honji Senjuji] * [http://www.senjuji.or.jp/ Senjuji]

revision.text:
{{Infobox organization | name = Shinshū Takada-ha | native_name = 真宗高田派 | image = [[File:Shinshu Takada-ha Shumuin.JPG|250px]] | caption = The Religious Affairs Office at the head temple [[Senjuji]] | type = [[Religious corporation]] | headquarters = 2819 Isshinden-chō, Tsu City, Mie Prefecture | leader_title = Hōshu (Head Priest) | leader_name = Jishō | website = {{URL|http://www.senjuji.or.jp/}} }}
'''Shinshū Takada-ha''' (真宗高田派) is a branch of {{ill|Jōdo Shinshū|ja|淡土真宗}}, with its head temple at {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺}} in {{ill|Tsu, Mie|ja|渡哥市}}. It traces its origins to the disciples {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏}} and {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智}}, who led the Highada-monto community in the medieval period. The sect has approximately 640 affiliated temples.
== History ==
=== Founding Period === In {{ill|1205|ja|1205年}}, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇}}, the practice of {{ill|Senju Nembutsu|ja|専修念仏}}, propagated by {{ill|Hōnen|ja|法然}} and his disciples, was banned, leading to the exile of {{ill|Shinran|ja|親熊}} to {{ill|Echigo Province|ja|越後国}}. After being pardoned in {{ill|1211|ja|1211年}}, Shinran traveled to {{ill|Zenkoji|ja|善光寺}} and then settled in {{ill|Hitachi Province|ja|常陽国}}, where he began his missionary activities.
Shinran established his base at {{ill|Nishi Nembutsuji|ja|西念仏寺}} in Hitachi, and in {{ill|1224|ja|1224年}}, he completed his seminal work, {{ill|Kyōgyōshinshō|ja|顯淡土真実教行証文類}} ('Teaching, Practice, Faith, and Attainment'), which laid the doctrinal foundation for Jōdo Shinshū.
In {{ill|1225|ja|1225年}}, according to tradition, Shinran received a revelation instructing him to establish a temple in Highada. With the support of {{ill|Ōuchi Clan|ja|大内氏}}, the ruler of {{ill|Mooka Castle|ja|真岡城}}, he enshrined a replica of the {{ill|Zenkoji-style Amida Triad|ja|善光寺式阿弥陀三宗}} in what became {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺}}, marking the origins of the Highada-monto tradition and the Shinshū Takada-ha sect.
The temple was officially named ''Senshū Amida-ji'' in {{ill|1226|ja|1226年}} by imperial decree, and Shinran spent about seven years there before returning to Kyoto. After Shinran's departure, his prominent disciple {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏}} succeeded him as the second head priest of Senjuji, followed by {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智}}.
=== Sengoku Period === During the 15th century, the sect expanded significantly under the leadership of the 10th head priest, {{ill|Shin'e|ja|真恵}}, who actively spread Jōdo Shinshū teachings across {{ill|Tōkai region|ja|東海地方}} and {{ill|Hokuriku region|ja|北陸地方}}. However, conflicts arose with {{ill|Rennyo|ja|蓮妃}}, the influential head of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺}}, leading many followers and temples to shift allegiance to the Honganji faction. In {{ill|1465|ja|1465年}}, Shin'e petitioned {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比士山}} to recognize Takada-ha as separate from Honganji, which was granted.
Despite setbacks due to military conflicts, including the {{ill|Echizen Ikkō-Ikki|ja|越前一向一挑}}, Takada-ha retained a strong presence in certain regions. By the 16th century, the sect moved its primary temple from {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}} to {{ill|Isshinden|ja|一身田}} in {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国}}, where it remains today.
=== Edo Period === During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Takada-ha maintained its status as one of the largest Jōdo Shinshū sects after the split of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺}} into the {{ill|Nishi Honganji|ja|西本願寺}} and {{ill|Higashi Honganji|ja|東本願寺}} branches. The sect continued to flourish, with its head temple, Senjuji, recognized as a ''jun-monzeki'' (quasi-imperial temple).
== Major Temples == ; Head Temple * {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺}} (Mooka, Tochigi Prefecture)
; Main Temple * {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺}} (Isshinden, Tsu, Mie Prefecture)
; Branch Temples * Kyoto Betsuin * {{ill|Nagoya Betsuin|ja|專修寺名古屋別院}} * Kobe Betsuin * Hokkaido Betsuin (Kiyota-ku, Sapporo, Hokkaido) * Fukui Betsuin * Kanto Betsuin * Yokohama Betsuin
== Characteristics == Takada-ha uniquely uses ''Bunrui-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of the Pure Land|ja|文類慰}}) for evening services, unlike other Jōdo Shinshū sects that primarily recite ''Shōshin-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of True Faith|ja|正信慰}}). Additionally, its temples traditionally do not use {{ill|Tannishō|ja|商異止}}, as the sect preserves a large collection of Shinran's original writings.
== External Links == * [http://www.honji-senjuji.jp/ Honji Senjuji] * [http://www.senjuji.or.jp/ Senjuji]
[[Category:Jōdo Shinshū sects]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist sects]] [[Category:Religious organizations in Japan]] [[ja:真宗高田派]]

== References == <references />
== Bibliography == * {{ill|Kuroda Toshio|en}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha-->[[ja:黒田俊雄]]. *Temple and Shrine Powers: Another Medieval Society.* Iwanami Shinsho, 1980. * {{ill|Itō Masatoshi|en}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha-->[[ja:伊藤正敏]]. *The Medieval Religious Powers: Migration and Connection.* Chikuma Shinsho, 2008. == See Also! == * {{ill|Kenmitsu System|ja|顕密体制|en}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha-->[[ja:顕密体制]] * {{ill|Kenmon System Theory|ja|権門体制|en}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha-->[[ja:権門体制]] * {{ill|Right of asylum|ja|アジール権|fr|Droit d'asile|en}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha-->[[ja:アジール]] * {{ill|Temple Precinct Cities|ja|境内都市|en}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha-->[[ja:境内都市]] * {{ill|Jinaimachi|fr|Jinaicho|ja|寺内町|lt=Teramachi|en}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha-->[[ja:寺内町]] * {{ill|Sōhei|en}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha-->[[ja:僧兵]] * {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha-->[[ja:神人]]

{{Infobox organization | name = Shinshū Takada-ha | native_name = 真宗高田派 | image = [[File:Shinshu Takada-ha Shumuin.JPG|250px]] | caption = The Religious Affairs Office at the head temple [[Senjuji]] | type = [[Religious corporation]] | headquarters = 2819 Isshinden-chō, Tsu City, Mie Prefecture | leader_title = Hōshu (Head Priest) | leader_name = Jishō | website = {{URL|http://www.senjuji.or.jp/}} }}
'''Shinshū Takada-ha''' (真宗高田派) is a branch of {{ill|Jōdo Shinshū|ja|淡土真宗}}, with its head temple at {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺}} in {{ill|Tsu, Mie|ja|渡哥市}}. It traces its origins to the disciples {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏}} and {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智}}, who led the Highada-monto community in the medieval period. The sect has approximately 640 affiliated temples.
== History ==
=== Founding Period === In {{ill|1205|ja|1205年}}, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇}}, the practice of {{ill|Senju Nembutsu|ja|専修念仏}}, propagated by {{ill|Hōnen|ja|法然}} and his disciples, was banned, leading to the exile of {{ill|Shinran|ja|親熊}} to {{ill|Echigo Province|ja|越後国}}. After being pardoned in {{ill|1211|ja|1211th year }}, Shinran traveled to {{ill|Zenkoji|ja|善光寺}} and then settled in {{ill|Hitachi Province|ja|常陽国}}, where he began his missionary activities.
Shinran established his base at {{ill|Nishi Nembutsuji|ja|西念仏寺}} in Hitachi, and in {{ill|1224|ja|1224年}}, he completed his seminal work, {{ill|Kyōgyōshinshō|ja|顯淡土真実教行証文類}} ('Teaching, Practice, Faith, and Attainment'), which laid the doctrinal foundation for Jōdo Shinshū.
In {{ill|1225|ja|1225年}}, according to tradition, Shinran received a revelation instructing him to establish a temple in Highada. With the support of {{ill|Ōuchi Clan|ja|大内氏}}, the ruler of {{ill|Mooka Castle|ja|真岡城}}, he enshrined a replica of the {{ill|Zenkoji-style Amida Triad|ja|善光寺式阿弥陀三宗}} in what became {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺}}, marking the origins of the Highada-monto tradition and the Shinshū Takada-ha sect.
The temple was officially named ''Senshū Amida-ji'' in {{ill|1226|ja|1226年}} by imperial decree, and Shinran spent about seven years there before returning to Kyoto. After Shinran's departure, his prominent disciple {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏}} succeeded him as the second head priest of Senjuji, followed by {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智}}.
=== Sengoku Period === During the 15th century, the sect expanded significantly under the leadership of the 10th head priest, {{ill|Shin'e|ja|真恵}}, who actively spread Jōdo Shinshū teachings across {{ill|Tōkai region|ja|東海地方}} and {{ill|Hokuriku region|ja|北陸地方}}. However, conflicts arose with {{ill|Rennyo|ja|蓮妃}}, the influential head of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺}}, leading many followers and temples to shift allegiance to the Honganji faction. In {{ill|1465|ja|1465年}}, Shin'e petitioned {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比士山}} to recognize Takada-ha as separate from Honganji, which was granted.
Despite setbacks due to military conflicts, including the {{ill|Echizen Ikkō-Ikki|ja|越前一向一挑}}, Takada-ha retained a strong presence in certain regions. By the 16th century, the sect moved its primary temple from {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}} to {{ill|Isshinden|ja|一身田}} in {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国}}, where it remains today.
=== Edo Period === During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Takada-ha maintained its status as one of the largest Jōdo Shinshū sects after the split of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺}} into the {{ill|Nishi Honganji|ja|西本願寺}} and {{ill|Higashi Honganji|ja|東本願寺}} branches. The sect continued to flourish, with its head temple, Senjuji, recognized as a ''jun-monzeki'' (quasi-imperial temple).
== Major Temples == ; Head Temple * {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺}} (Mooka, Tochigi Prefecture)
; Main Temple * {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺}} (Isshinden, Tsu, Mie Prefecture)
; Branch Temples * Kyoto Betsuin * {{ill|Nagoya Betsuin|ja|專修寺名古屋別院}} * Kobe Betsuin * Hokkaido Betsuin (Kiyota-ku, Sapporo, Hokkaido) * Fukui Betsuin * Kanto Betsuin * Yokohama Betsuin
== Characteristics == Takada-ha uniquely uses ''Bunrui-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of the Pure Land|ja|文類慰}}) for evening services, unlike other Jōdo Shinshū sects that primarily recite ''Shōshin-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of True Faith|ja|正信慰}}). Additionally, its temples traditionally do not use {{ill|Tannishō|ja|商異止}}, as the sect preserves a large collection of Shinran's original writings.
== External Links == * [http://www.honji-senjuji.jp/ Honji Senjuji] * [http://www.senjuji.or.jp/ Senjuji]

With the support of {{ill|Ōuchi Clan|ja|大内氏}}, the ruler of {{ill|Mooka Castle|ja|真岡城}}, he enshrined a replica of the {{ill|Zenkoji-style Amida Triad|ja|善光寺式阿弥陀三宗}} in what became {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺}}, marking the origins of the Highada-monto tradition and the Shinshū Takada-ha sect.
The temple was officially named ''Senshū Amida-ji'' in {{ill|1226|ja|1226年}} by imperial decree, and Shinran spent about seven years there before returning to Kyoto. After Shinran's departure, his prominent disciple {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏}} succeeded him as the second head priest of Senjuji, followed by {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智}}.
=== Sengoku Period === During the 15th century, the sect expanded significantly under the leadership of the 10th head priest, {{ill|Shin'e|ja|真恵}}, who actively spread Jōdo Shinshū teachings across {{ill|Tōkai region|ja|東海地方}} and {{ill|Hokuriku region|ja|北陸地方}}. However, conflicts arose with {{ill|Rennyo|ja|蓮妃}}, the influential head of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺}}, leading many followers and temples to shift allegiance to the Honganji faction. In {{ill|1465|ja|1465th year }}, Shin'e petitioned {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比士山}} to recognize Takada-ha as separate from Honganji, which was granted.
Despite setbacks due to military conflicts, including the {{ill|Echizen Ikkō-Ikki|ja|越前一向一挑}}, Takada-ha retained a strong presence in certain regions. By the 16th century, the sect moved its primary temple from {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}} to {{ill|Isshinden|ja|一身田}} in {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国}}, where it remains today.
=== Edo Period === During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Takada-ha maintained its status as one of the largest Jōdo Shinshū sects after the split of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺}} into the {{ill|Nishi Honganji|ja|西本願寺}} and {{ill|Higashi Honganji|ja|東本願寺}} branches. The sect continued to flourish, with its head temple, Senjuji, recognized as a ''jun-monzeki'' (quasi-imperial temple).
== Major Temples == ; Head Temple * {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺}} (Mooka, Tochigi Prefecture)
; Main Temple * {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺}} (Isshinden, Tsu, Mie Prefecture)
; Branch Temples * Kyoto Betsuin * {{ill|Nagoya Betsuin|ja|專修寺名古屋別院}} * Kobe Betsuin * Hokkaido Betsuin (Kiyota-ku, Sapporo, Hokkaido) * Fukui Betsuin * Kanto Betsuin * Yokohama Betsuin
== Characteristics == Takada-ha uniquely uses ''Bunrui-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of the Pure Land|ja|文類慰}}) for evening services, unlike other Jōdo Shinshū sects that primarily recite ''Shōshin-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of True Faith|ja|正信慰}}). Additionally, its temples traditionally do not use {{ill|Tannishō|ja|商異止}}, as the sect preserves a large collection of Shinran's original writings.
== External Links == * [http://www.honji-senjuji.jp/ Honji Senjuji] * [http://www.senjuji.or.jp/ Senjuji]

It traces its origins to the disciples {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏}} and {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智}}, who led the Highada-monto community in the medieval period. The sect has approximately 640 affiliated temples.

{{Infobox organization | name = Shinshū Takada-ha | native_name = 真宗高田派 | image = [[File:Shinshu Takada-ha Shumuin.JPG|250px]] | caption = The Religious Affairs Office at the head temple [[Senjuji]] | type = [[Religious corporation]] | headquarters = 2819 Isshinden-chō, Tsu City, Mie Prefecture | leader_title = Hōshu (Head Priest) | leader_name = Jishō | website = {{URL|http://www.senjuji.or.jp/}} }}
'''Shinshū Takada-ha''' (真宗高田派) is a branch of {{ill|Jōdo Shinshū|ja|淡土真宗}}, with its head temple at {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺}} in {{ill|Tsu, Mie|ja|渡哥市}}. It traces its origins to the disciples {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏}} and {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智}}, who led the Highada-monto community in the medieval period. The sect has approximately 640 affiliated temples.
== History ==
=== Founding Period === In {{ill|1205|ja|1205th year }}, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇}}, the practice of {{ill|Senju Nembutsu|ja|専修念仏}}, propagated by {{ill|Hōnen|ja|法然}} and his disciples, was banned, leading to the exile of {{ill|Shinran|ja|親熊}} to {{ill|Echigo Province|ja|越後国}}. After being pardoned in {{ill|1211|ja|1211th year }}, Shinran traveled to {{ill|Zenkoji|ja|善光寺}} and then settled in {{ill|Hitachi Province|ja|常陽国}}, where he began his missionary activities.
Shinran established his base at {{ill|Nishi Nembutsuji|ja|西念仏寺}} in Hitachi, and in {{ill|1224|ja|1224th year }}, he completed his seminal work, {{ill|Kyōgyōshinshō|ja|顯淡土真実教行証文類}} ('Teaching, Practice, Faith, and Attainment'), which laid the doctrinal foundation for Jōdo Shinshū.
In {{ill|1225|ja|1225th year }}, according to tradition, Shinran received a revelation instructing him to establish a temple in Highada. With the support of {{ill|Ōuchi Clan|ja|大内氏}}, the ruler of {{ill|Mooka Castle|ja|真岡城}}, he enshrined a replica of the {{ill|Zenkoji-style Amida Triad|ja|善光寺式阿弥陀三宗}} in what became {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺}}, marking the origins of the Highada-monto tradition and the Shinshū Takada-ha sect.
The temple was officially named ''Senshū Amida-ji'' in {{ill|1226|ja|1226年}} by imperial decree, and Shinran spent about seven years there before returning to Kyoto. After Shinran's departure, his prominent disciple {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏}} succeeded him as the second head priest of Senjuji, followed by {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智}}.
=== Sengoku Period === During the 15th century, the sect expanded significantly under the leadership of the 10th head priest, {{ill|Shin'e|ja|真恵}}, who actively spread Jōdo Shinshū teachings across {{ill|Tōkai region|ja|東海地方}} and {{ill|Hokuriku region|ja|北陸地方}}. However, conflicts arose with {{ill|Rennyo|ja|蓮妃}}, the influential head of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺}}, leading many followers and temples to shift allegiance to the Honganji faction. In {{ill|1465|ja|1465th year }}, Shin'e petitioned {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比士山}} to recognize Takada-ha as separate from Honganji, which was granted.
Despite setbacks due to military conflicts, including the {{ill|Echizen Ikkō-Ikki|ja|越前一向一挑}}, Takada-ha retained a strong presence in certain regions. By the 16th century, the sect moved its primary temple from {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}} to {{ill|Isshinden|ja|一身田}} in {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国}}, where it remains today.
=== Edo Period === During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Takada-ha maintained its status as one of the largest Jōdo Shinshū sects after the split of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺}} into the {{ill|Nishi Honganji|ja|西本願寺}} and {{ill|Higashi Honganji|ja|東本願寺}} branches. The sect continued to flourish, with its head temple, Senjuji, recognized as a ''jun-monzeki'' (quasi-imperial temple).
== Major Temples == ; Head Temple * {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺}} (Mooka, Tochigi Prefecture)
; Main Temple * {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺}} (Isshinden, Tsu, Mie Prefecture)
; Branch Temples * Kyoto Betsuin * {{ill|Nagoya Betsuin|ja|專修寺名古屋別院}} * Kobe Betsuin * Hokkaido Betsuin (Kiyota-ku, Sapporo, Hokkaido) * Fukui Betsuin * Kanto Betsuin * Yokohama Betsuin
== Characteristics == Takada-ha uniquely uses ''Bunrui-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of the Pure Land|ja|文類慰}}) for evening services, unlike other Jōdo Shinshū sects that primarily recite ''Shōshin-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of True Faith|ja|正信慰}}). Additionally, its temples traditionally do not use {{ill|Tannishō|ja|商異止}}, as the sect preserves a large collection of Shinran's original writings.
== External Links == * [http://www.honji-senjuji.jp/ Honji Senjuji] * [http://www.senjuji.or.jp/ Senjuji]

revision.text:
{{Infobox organization | name = Shinshū Takada-ha | native_name = 真宗高田派 | image = [[File:Shinshu Takada-ha Shumuin.JPG|250px]] | caption = The Religious Affairs Office at the head temple [[Senjuji]] | type = [[Religious corporation]] | headquarters = 2819 Isshinden-chō, Tsu City, Mie Prefecture | leader_title = Hōshu (Head Priest) | leader_name = Jishō | website = {{URL|http://www.senjuji.or.jp/}} }}
'''Shinshū Takada-ha''' (真宗高田派) is a branch of {{ill|Jōdo Shinshū|ja|淡土真宗|12=draft}}, with its head temple at {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=draft}} in {{ill|Tsu, Mie|ja|渡哥市|12=draft}}. It traces its origins to the disciples {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智|12=draft}}, who led the Highada-monto community in the medieval period. The sect has approximately 640 affiliated temples.
== History ==
=== Founding Period === In {{ill|1205|ja|1205th year|12=draft}}, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇|12=draft}}, the practice of {{ill|Senju Nembutsu|ja|専修念仏|12=draft}}, propagated by {{ill|Hōnen|ja|法然|12=draft}} and his disciples, was banned, leading to the exile of {{ill|Shinran|ja|親熊|12=draft}} to {{ill|Echigo Province|ja|越後国|12=draft}}. After being pardoned in {{ill|1211|ja|1211th year|12=draft}}, Shinran traveled to {{ill|Zenkoji|ja|善光寺|12=draft}} and then settled in {{ill|Hitachi Province|ja|常陽国|12=draft}}, where he began his missionary activities.
Shinran established his base at {{ill|Nishi Nembutsuji|ja|西念仏寺|12=draft}} in Hitachi, and in {{ill|1224|ja|1224th year|12=draft}}, he completed his seminal work, {{ill|Kyōgyōshinshō|ja|顯淡土真実教行証文類|12=draft}} ('Teaching, Practice, Faith, and Attainment'), which laid the doctrinal foundation for Jōdo Shinshū.
In {{ill|1225|ja|1225th year|12=draft}}, according to tradition, Shinran received a revelation instructing him to establish a temple in Highada. With the support of {{ill|Ōuchi Clan|ja|大内氏|12=draft}}, the ruler of {{ill|Mooka Castle|ja|真岡城|12=draft}}, he enshrined a replica of the {{ill|Zenkoji-style Amida Triad|ja|善光寺式阿弥陀三宗|12=draft}} in what became {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=draft}}, marking the origins of the Highada-monto tradition and the Shinshū Takada-ha sect.
The temple was officially named ''Senshū Amida-ji'' in {{ill|1226|ja|1226年|12=draft}} by imperial decree, and Shinran spent about seven years there before returning to Kyoto. After Shinran's departure, his prominent disciple {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏|12=draft}} succeeded him as the second head priest of Senjuji, followed by {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智|12=draft}}.
=== Sengoku Period === During the 15th century, the sect expanded significantly under the leadership of the 10th head priest, {{ill|Shin'e|ja|真恵|12=draft}}, who actively spread Jōdo Shinshū teachings across {{ill|Tōkai region|ja|東海地方|12=draft}} and {{ill|Hokuriku region|ja|北陸地方|12=draft}}. However, conflicts arose with {{ill|Rennyo|ja|蓮妃|12=draft}}, the influential head of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺|12=draft}}, leading many followers and temples to shift allegiance to the Honganji faction. In {{ill|1465|ja|1465th year|12=draft}}, Shin'e petitioned {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比士山|12=draft}} to recognize Takada-ha as separate from Honganji, which was granted.
Despite setbacks due to military conflicts, including the {{ill|Echizen Ikkō-Ikki|ja|越前一向一挑|12=draft}}, Takada-ha retained a strong presence in certain regions. By the 16th century, the sect moved its primary temple from {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国|12=draft}} to {{ill|Isshinden|ja|一身田|12=draft}} in {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国|12=draft}}, where it remains today.
=== Edo Period === During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, Takada-ha maintained its status as one of the largest Jōdo Shinshū sects after the split of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺|12=draft}} into the {{ill|Nishi Honganji|ja|西本願寺|12=draft}} and {{ill|Higashi Honganji|ja|東本願寺|12=draft}} branches. The sect continued to flourish, with its head temple, Senjuji, recognized as a ''jun-monzeki'' (quasi-imperial temple).
== Major Temples == ; Head Temple * {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=draft}} (Mooka, Tochigi Prefecture)
; Main Temple * {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=draft}} (Isshinden, Tsu, Mie Prefecture)
; Branch Temples * Kyoto Betsuin * {{ill|Nagoya Betsuin|ja|專修寺名古屋別院|12=draft}} * Kobe Betsuin * Hokkaido Betsuin (Kiyota-ku, Sapporo, Hokkaido) * Fukui Betsuin * Kanto Betsuin * Yokohama Betsuin
== Characteristics == Takada-ha uniquely uses ''Bunrui-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of the Pure Land|ja|文類慰|12=draft}}) for evening services, unlike other Jōdo Shinshū sects that primarily recite ''Shōshin-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of True Faith|ja|正信慰|12=draft}}). Additionally, its temples traditionally do not use {{ill|Tannishō|ja|商異止|12=draft}}, as the sect preserves a large collection of Shinran's original writings.
== External Links == * [http://www.honji-senjuji.jp/ Honji Senjuji] * [http://www.senjuji.or.jp/ Senjuji]
) * {{ill|Kimpusen-ji|en}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha--> (Yoshino) * {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|en|12=draft}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha--> * {{ill|Daigo-ji|en}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha--> * {{ill|Kongōbu-ji|en|12=draft}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha--> ({{ill|Mount Kōya|en}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha-->) * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|en|12=draft}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha--> * {{ill|Shōgoin|en}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha--> * {{ill|Onjō-ji|en|12=draft}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha--> * {{ill|Negoro-ji|en}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha--> * {{ill|Dewa Sanzan|en|12=draft}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha--> * {{ill|Heisen-ji|en}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha--> (now {{ill|Heisen-ji Hakusan Shrine|en|12=draft}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha-->) * {{ill|Isurugi-yama|en}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha--> Tenpyō-ji (now {{ill|Isurugi Hikojinja|en|12=draft}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha-->) * {{ill|Tōnomine|en}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha--> Myōraku-ji (now {{ill|Tanzan Shrine|en|12=draft}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha-->) * {{ill|Ōyama-dera|en}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha--> * {{ill|Suwa-taisha|en|12=draft}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha--> * {{ill|Itsukushima Shrine|en}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha--> * {{ill|Munakata Taisha|en|12=draft}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha--> * {{ill|Usa Jingū|en}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha--> * {{ill|Aso Shrine|en|12=draft}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha--> * {{ill|Hiko-san|en}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha--> Reisō-ji
== References == <references />
== Bibliography == * {{ill|Kuroda Toshio|en|12=draft}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha-->. *Temple and Shrine Powers: Another Medieval Society.* Iwanami Shinsho, 1980. * {{ill|Itō Masatoshi|en}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha-->. *The Medieval Religious Powers: Migration and Connection.* Chikuma Shinsho, 2008. == See Also == * {{ill|Kenmitsu System|ja|顕密体制|en|12=draft}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha--> * {{ill|Kenmon System Theory|ja|権門体制|en|12=draft}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha--> * {{ill|Right of asylum|ja|アジール権|fr|Droit d'asile|en|12=draft}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha--> * {{ill|Temple Precinct Cities|ja|境内都市|en|12=draft}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha--> * {{ill|Jinaimachi|fr|Jinaicho|ja|寺内町|lt=Teramachi|en|12=draft}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha--> * {{ill|Sōhei|en}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha--> * {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人|12=draft}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha-->

{{Infobox organization | name = Shinshū Takada-ha | native_name = 真宗高田派 | image = [[File:Shinshu Takada-ha Shumuin.JPG|250px]] | caption = The Religious Affairs Office at the head temple [[Senjuji]] | type = [[Religious corporation]] | headquarters = 2819 Isshinden-chō, Tsu City, Mie Prefecture | leader_title = Hōshu (Head Priest) | leader_name = Jishō | website = {{URL|http://www.senjuji.or.jp/}} }}
'''Shinshū Takada-ha''' (真宗高田派) is a branch of {{ill|Jōdo Shinshū|ja|淡土真宗|12=draft}}, with its head temple at {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=draft}} in {{ill|Tsu, Mie|ja|渡哥市|12=draft}}. It traces its origins to the disciples {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智|12=draft}}, who led the Highada-monto community in the medieval period. The sect has approximately 640 affiliated temples.
== History ==
=== Founding Period === In {{ill|1205|ja|1205th year|12=draft}}, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇|12=draft}}, the practice of {{ill|Senju Nembutsu|ja|専修念仏|12=draft}}, propagated by {{ill|Hōnen|ja|法然|12=draft}} and his disciples, was banned, leading to the exile of {{ill|Shinran|ja|親熊|12=draft}} to {{ill|Echigo Province|ja|越後国|12=draft}}. After being pardoned in {{ill|1211|ja|1211th year|12=draft}}, Shinran traveled to {{ill|Zenkoji|ja|善光寺|12=draft}} and then settled in {{ill|Hitachi Province|ja|常陽国|12=draft}}, where he began his missionary activities.
Shinran established his base at {{ill|Nishi Nembutsuji|ja|西念仏寺|12=draft}} in Hitachi, and in {{ill|1224|ja|1224th year|12=draft}}, he completed his seminal work, {{ill|Kyōgyōshinshō|ja|顯淡土真実教行証文類|12=draft}} ('Teaching, Practice, Faith, and Attainment'), which laid the doctrinal foundation for Jōdo Shinshū.
In {{ill|1225|ja|1225th year|12=draft}}, according to tradition, Shinran received a revelation instructing him to establish a temple in Highada. With the support of {{ill|Ōuchi Clan|ja|大内氏|12=draft}}, the ruler of {{ill|Mooka Castle|ja|真岡城|12=draft}}, he enshrined a replica of the {{ill|Zenkoji-style Amida Triad|ja|善光寺式阿弥陀三宗|12=draft}} in what became {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=draft}}, marking the origins of the Highada-monto tradition and the Shinshū Takada-ha sect.
The temple was officially named ''Senshū Amida-ji'' in {{ill|1226|ja|1226年|12=draft}} by imperial decree, and Shinran spent about seven years there before returning to Kyoto. After Shinran's departure, his prominent disciple {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏|12=draft}} succeeded him as the second head priest of Senjuji, followed by {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智|12=draft}}.
=== Sengoku Period === During the 15th century, the sect expanded significantly under the leadership of the 10th head priest, {{ill|Shin'e|ja|真恵|12=draft}}, who actively spread Jōdo Shinshū teachings across {{ill|Tōkai region|ja|東海地方|12=draft}} and {{ill|Hokuriku region|ja|北陸地方|12=draft}}. However, conflicts arose with {{ill|Rennyo|ja|蓮妃|12=draft}}, the influential head of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺|12=draft}}, leading many followers and temples to shift allegiance to the Honganji faction. In {{ill|1465|ja|1465th year|12=draft}}, Shin'e petitioned {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比士山|12=draft}} to recognize Takada-ha as separate from Honganji, which was granted.
Despite setbacks due to military conflicts, including the {{ill|Echizen Ikkō-Ikki|ja|越前一向一挑|12=draft}}, Takada-ha retained a strong presence in certain regions. By the 16th century, the sect moved its primary temple from {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国|12=draft}} to {{ill|Isshinden|ja|一身田|12=draft}} in {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国|12=draft}}, where it remains today.
=== Edo Period === During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, Takada-ha maintained its status as one of the largest Jōdo Shinshū sects after the split of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺|12=draft}} into the {{ill|Nishi Honganji|ja|西本願寺|12=draft}} and {{ill|Higashi Honganji|ja|東本願寺|12=draft}} branches. The sect continued to flourish, with its head temple, Senjuji, recognized as a ''jun-monzeki'' (quasi-imperial temple).
== Major Temples == ; Head Temple * {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=draft}} (Mooka, Tochigi Prefecture)
; Main Temple * {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=draft}} (Isshinden, Tsu, Mie Prefecture)
; Branch Temples * Kyoto Betsuin * {{ill|Nagoya Betsuin|ja|專修寺名古屋別院|12=draft}} * Kobe Betsuin * Hokkaido Betsuin (Kiyota-ku, Sapporo, Hokkaido) * Fukui Betsuin * Kanto Betsuin * Yokohama Betsuin
== Characteristics == Takada-ha uniquely uses ''Bunrui-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of the Pure Land|ja|文類慰|12=draft}}) for evening services, unlike other Jōdo Shinshū sects that primarily recite ''Shōshin-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of True Faith|ja|正信慰|12=draft}}). Additionally, its temples traditionally do not use {{ill|Tannishō|ja|商異止|12=draft}}, as the sect preserves a large collection of Shinran's original writings.
== External Links == * [http://www.honji-senjuji.jp/ Honji Senjuji] * [http://www.senjuji.or.jp/ Senjuji] ) * {{ill|Kimpusen-ji|en}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha--> (Yoshino) * {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|en|12=draft}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha--> * {{ill|Daigo-ji|en}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha--> * {{ill|Kongōbu-ji|en|12=draft}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha--> ({{ill|Mount Kōya|en}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha-->) * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|en|12=draft}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha--> * {{ill|Shōgoin|en}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha--> * {{ill|Onjō-ji|en|12=draft}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha--> * {{ill|Negoro-ji|en}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha--> * {{ill|Dewa Sanzan|en|12=draft}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha--> * {{ill|Heisen-ji|en}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha--> (now {{ill|Heisen-ji Hakusan Shrine|en|12=draft}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha-->) * {{ill|Isurugi-yama|en}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha--> Tenpyō-ji (now {{ill|Isurugi Hikojinja|en|12=draft}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha-->) * {{ill|Tōnomine|en}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha--> Myōraku-ji (now {{ill|Tanzan Shrine|en|12=draft}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha-->) * {{ill|Ōyama-dera|en}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha--> * {{ill|Suwa-taisha|en|12=draft}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha--> * {{ill|Itsukushima Shrine|en}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha--> * {{ill|Munakata Taisha|en|12=draft}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha--> * {{ill|Usa Jingū|en}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha--> * {{ill|Aso Shrine|en|12=draft}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha--> * {{ill|Hiko-san|en}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha--> Reisō-ji
== References == <references />
== Bibliography == * {{ill|Kuroda Toshio|en|12=draft}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha-->. *Temple and Shrine Powers: Another Medieval Society.* Iwanami Shinsho, 1980. * {{ill|Itō Masatoshi|en}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha-->. *The Medieval Religious Powers: Migration and Connection.* Chikuma Shinsho, 2008. == See Also == * {{ill|Kenmitsu System|ja|顕密体制|en|12=draft}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha--> * {{ill|Kenmon System Theory|ja|権門体制|en|12=draft}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha--> * {{ill|Right of asylum|ja|アジール権|fr|Droit d'asile|en|12=draft}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha--> * {{ill|Temple Precinct Cities|ja|境内都市|en|12=draft}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha--> * {{ill|Jinaimachi|fr|Jinaicho|ja|寺内町|lt=Teramachi|en|12=draft}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha--> * {{ill|Sōhei|en}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha--> * {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人|12=draft}} <!--Shinshū Takada-ha-->

By the 16th century, the sect moved its primary temple from {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国|12=draft}} to {{ill|Isshinden|ja|一身田|12=draft}} in {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国|12=draft}}, where it remains today.
=== Edo Period === During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, Takada-ha maintained its status as one of the largest Jōdo Shinshū sects after the split of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺|12=draft}} into the {{ill|Nishi Honganji|ja|西本願寺|12=draft}} and {{ill|Higashi Honganji|ja|東本願寺|12=draft}} branches. The sect continued to flourish, with its head temple, Senjuji, recognized as a ''jun-monzeki'' (quasi-imperial temple).
== Major Temples == ; Head Temple * {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=draft}} (Mooka, Tochigi Prefecture)
; Main Temple * {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=draft}} (Isshinden, Tsu, Mie Prefecture)
; Branch Temples * Kyoto Betsuin * {{ill|Nagoya Betsuin|ja|專修寺名古屋別院|12=draft}} * Kobe Betsuin * Hokkaido Betsuin (Kiyota-ku, Sapporo, Hokkaido) * Fukui Betsuin * Kanto Betsuin * Yokohama Betsuin
== Characteristics == Takada-ha uniquely uses ''Bunrui-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of the Pure Land|ja|文類慰|12=draft}}) for evening services, unlike other Jōdo Shinshū sects that primarily recite ''Shōshin-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of True Faith|ja|正信慰|12=draft}}). Additionally, its temples traditionally do not use {{ill|Tannishō|ja|商異止|12=draft}}, as the sect preserves a large collection of Shinran's original writings.

{{Infobox organization | name = Shinshū Takada-ha | native_name = 真宗高田派 | image = [[File:Shinshu Takada-ha Shumuin.JPG|250px]] | caption = The Religious Affairs Office at the head temple [[Senjuji]] | type = [[Religious corporation]] | headquarters = 2819 Isshinden-chō, Tsu City, Mie Prefecture | leader_title = Hōshu (Head Priest) | leader_name = Jishō | website = {{URL|http://www.senjuji.or.jp/}} }}
'''Shinshū Takada-ha''' (真宗高田派) is a branch of {{ill|Jōdo Shinshū|ja|淡土真宗|12=draft}}, with its head temple at {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=draft}} in {{ill|Tsu, Mie|ja|渡哥市|12=draft}}. It traces its origins to the disciples {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智|12=draft}}, who led the Highada-monto community in the medieval period. The sect has approximately 640 affiliated temples.
== History ==
=== Founding Period === In {{ill|1205|ja|1205th year|12=draft}}, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇|12=draft}}, the practice of {{ill|Senju Nembutsu|ja|専修念仏|12=draft}}, propagated by {{ill|Hōnen|ja|法然|12=draft}} and his disciples, was banned, leading to the exile of {{ill|Shinran|ja|親熊|12=draft}} to {{ill|Echigo Province|ja|越後国|12=draft}}. After being pardoned in {{ill|1211|ja|1211th year|12=draft}}, Shinran traveled to {{ill|Zenkoji|ja|善光寺|12=draft}} and then settled in {{ill|Hitachi Province|ja|常陽国|12=draft}}, where he began his missionary activities.
Shinran established his base at {{ill|Nishi Nembutsuji|ja|西念仏寺|12=draft}} in Hitachi, and in {{ill|1224|ja|1224th year|12=draft}}, he completed his seminal work, {{ill|Kyōgyōshinshō|ja|顯淡土真実教行証文類|12=draft}} ('Teaching, Practice, Faith, and Attainment'), which laid the doctrinal foundation for Jōdo Shinshū.
In {{ill|1225|ja|1225th year|12=draft}}, according to tradition, Shinran received a revelation instructing him to establish a temple in Highada. With the support of {{ill|Ōuchi Clan|ja|大内氏|12=draft}}, the ruler of {{ill|Mooka Castle|ja|真岡城|12=draft}}, he enshrined a replica of the {{ill|Zenkoji-style Amida Triad|ja|善光寺式阿弥陀三宗|12=draft}} in what became {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=draft}}, marking the origins of the Highada-monto tradition and the Shinshū Takada-ha sect.
The temple was officially named ''Senshū Amida-ji'' in {{ill|1226|ja|1226年|12=draft}} by imperial decree, and Shinran spent about seven years there before returning to Kyoto. After Shinran's departure, his prominent disciple {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏|12=draft}} succeeded him as the second head priest of Senjuji, followed by {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智|12=draft}}.
=== Sengoku Period === During the 15th century, the sect expanded significantly under the leadership of the 10th head priest, {{ill|Shin'e|ja|真恵|12=draft}}, who actively spread Jōdo Shinshū teachings across {{ill|Tōkai region|ja|東海地方|12=draft}} and {{ill|Hokuriku region|ja|北陸地方|12=draft}}. However, conflicts arose with {{ill|Rennyo|ja|蓮妃|12=draft}}, the influential head of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺|12=draft}}, leading many followers and temples to shift allegiance to the Honganji faction. In {{ill|1465|ja|1465th year|12=draft}}, Shin'e petitioned {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比士山|12=draft}} to recognize Takada-ha as separate from Honganji, which was granted.
Despite setbacks due to military conflicts, including the {{ill|Echizen Ikkō-Ikki|ja|越前一向一挑|12=draft}}, Takada-ha retained a strong presence in certain regions. By the 16th century, the sect moved its primary temple from {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国|12=draft}} to {{ill|Isshinden|ja|一身田|12=draft}} in {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国|12=draft}}, where it remains today.
=== Edo Period === During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, Takada-ha maintained its status as one of the largest Jōdo Shinshū sects after the split of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺|12=draft}} into the {{ill|Nishi Honganji|ja|西本願寺|12=draft}} and {{ill|Higashi Honganji|ja|東本願寺|12=draft}} branches. The sect continued to flourish, with its head temple, Senjuji, recognized as a ''jun-monzeki'' (quasi-imperial temple).
== Major Temples == ; Head Temple * {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=draft}} (Mooka, Tochigi Prefecture)
; Main Temple * {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=draft}} (Isshinden, Tsu, Mie Prefecture)
; Branch Temples * Kyoto Betsuin * {{ill|Nagoya Betsuin|ja|專修寺名古屋別院|12=draft}} * Kobe Betsuin * Hokkaido Betsuin (Kiyota-ku, Sapporo, Hokkaido) * Fukui Betsuin * Kanto Betsuin * Yokohama Betsuin
== Characteristics == Takada-ha uniquely uses ''Bunrui-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of the Pure Land|ja|文類慰|12=draft}}) for evening services, unlike other Jōdo Shinshū sects that primarily recite ''Shōshin-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of True Faith|ja|正信慰|12=draft}}). Additionally, its temples traditionally do not use {{ill|Tannishō|ja|商異止|12=draft}}, as the sect preserves a large collection of Shinran's original writings.
