'''Shinshū Takada-ha''' (真宗高田派) is a branch of {{ill|Jōdo Shinshū|ja|淡土真宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōdo Shinshū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, with its head temple at {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Honji Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}} in {{ill|Tsu, Mie|ja|渡哥市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsu, Mie|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It traces its origins to the disciples {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu|1=Shinbutsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6606696}} and {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenchi|1=Kenchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6605658}}, who led the Highada-monto community in the medieval period. The sect has approximately 640 affiliated temples.

== History ==

=== Founding Period ===
In [[:en:1205|1205]], during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Go-Toba]], the practice of {{ill|Senju Nembutsu|ja|専修念仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senju Nembutsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 称名念仏|1=chengming nianfo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11596185}}, propagated by [[:en:Hōnen|Hōnen]] and his disciples, was banned, leading to the exile of {{ill|Shinran|ja|親熊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinran|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to [[:en:Echigo Province|Echigo Province]]. After being pardoned in [[:en:1211|1211]], Shinran traveled to [[:en:Zenkō-ji|Zenkoji]] and then settled in {{ill|Hitachi Province|ja|常陽国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitachi Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where he began his missionary activities.

Shinran established his base at {{ill|Nishi Nembutsuji|ja|西念仏寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishi Nembutsuji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Hitachi, and in [[:en:1224|1224]], he completed his seminal work, {{ill|Kyōgyōshinshō|ja|顯淡土真実教行証文類|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōgyōshinshō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ('Teaching, Practice, Faith, and Attainment'), which laid the doctrinal foundation for Jōdo Shinshū.

In [[:en:1225|1225]], according to tradition, Shinran received a revelation instructing him to establish a temple in Highada. With the support of [[:en:Ōuchi clan|Ōuchi Clan]], the ruler of {{ill|Mooka Castle|ja|真岡城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mooka Castle|1=Mooka Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11583106}}, he enshrined a replica of the {{ill|Zenkoji-style Amida Triad|ja|善光寺式阿弥陀三宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenkoji-style Amida Triad|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in what became {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Honji Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}}, marking the origins of the Highada-monto tradition and the Shinshū Takada-ha sect.

The temple was officially named ''Senshū Amida-ji'' in [[:en:1226|1226]] by imperial decree, and Shinran spent about seven years there before returning to Kyoto. After Shinran's departure, his prominent disciple {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu|1=Shinbutsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6606696}} succeeded him as the second head priest of Senjuji, followed by {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenchi|1=Kenchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6605658}}.

=== Sengoku Period ===
During the 15th century, the sect expanded significantly under the leadership of the 10th head priest, {{ill|Shin'e|ja|真恵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin'e|1=Shin'e|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q85271929}}, who actively spread Jōdo Shinshū teachings across [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai region]] and [[:en:Hokuriku region|Hokuriku region]]. However, conflicts arose with {{ill|Rennyo|ja|蓮妃|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rennyo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the influential head of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honganji|1=Hongan-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11521048}}, leading many followers and temples to shift allegiance to the Honganji faction. In [[:en:1465|1465]], Shin'e petitioned {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比士山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Hiei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to recognize Takada-ha as separate from Honganji, which was granted.

Despite setbacks due to military conflicts, including the {{ill|Echizen Ikkō-Ikki|ja|越前一向一挑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echizen Ikkō-Ikki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Takada-ha retained a strong presence in certain regions. By the 16th century, the sect moved its primary temple from [[:en:Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke Province]] to {{ill|Isshinden|ja|一身田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isshinden|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]], where it remains today.

=== Edo Period ===
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Takada-ha maintained its status as one of the largest Jōdo Shinshū sects after the split of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honganji|1=Hongan-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11521048}} into the [[:en:Nishi Hongan-ji|Nishi Honganji]] and [[:en:Higashi Hongan-ji|Higashi Honganji]] branches. The sect continued to flourish, with its head temple, Senjuji, recognized as a ''jun-monzeki'' (quasi-imperial temple).

* {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Honji Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}} (Isshinden, Tsu, Mie Prefecture)

Additionally, its temples traditionally do not use {{ill|Tannishō|ja|商異止|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tannishō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, as the sect preserves a large collection of Shinran's original writings.

{{Infobox organization
| name = Shinshū Takada-ha
| native_name = 真宗高田派
| image = [[File:Shinshu Takada-ha Shumuin.JPG|250px]]
| caption = The Religious Affairs Office at the head temple [[:en:Senjuji|Senjuji]]
| type = [[Religious corporation]]
| headquarters = 2819 Isshinden-chō, Tsu City, Mie Prefecture
| leader_title = Hōshu (Head Priest)
| leader_name = Jishō
| website = {{URL|http://www.senjuji.or.jp/}}
}}

'''Shinshū Takada-ha''' (真宗高田派) is a branch of {{ill|Jōdo Shinshū|ja|淡土真宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōdo Shinshū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, with its head temple at {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Honji Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}} in {{ill|Tsu, Mie|ja|渡哥市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsu, Mie|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It traces its origins to the disciples {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu|1=Shinbutsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6606696}} and {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenchi|1=Kenchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6605658}}, who led the Highada-monto community in the medieval period. The sect has approximately 640 affiliated temples.

== History ==

=== Founding Period ===
In [[:en:1205|1205]], during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Go-Toba]], the practice of {{ill|Senju Nembutsu|ja|専修念仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senju Nembutsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 称名念仏|1=chengming nianfo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11596185}}, propagated by [[:en:Hōnen|Hōnen]] and his disciples, was banned, leading to the exile of {{ill|Shinran|ja|親熊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinran|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to [[:en:Echigo Province|Echigo Province]]. After being pardoned in [[:en:1211|1211]], Shinran traveled to [[:en:Zenkō-ji|Zenkoji]] and then settled in {{ill|Hitachi Province|ja|常陽国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitachi Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where he began his missionary activities.

Shinran established his base at {{ill|Nishi Nembutsuji|ja|西念仏寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishi Nembutsuji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Hitachi, and in [[:en:1224|1224]], he completed his seminal work, {{ill|Kyōgyōshinshō|ja|顯淡土真実教行証文類|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōgyōshinshō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ('Teaching, Practice, Faith, and Attainment'), which laid the doctrinal foundation for Jōdo Shinshū.

In [[:en:1225|1225]], according to tradition, Shinran received a revelation instructing him to establish a temple in Highada. With the support of [[:en:Ōuchi clan|Ōuchi Clan]], the ruler of {{ill|Mooka Castle|ja|真岡城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mooka Castle|1=Mooka Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11583106}}, he enshrined a replica of the {{ill|Zenkoji-style Amida Triad|ja|善光寺式阿弥陀三宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenkoji-style Amida Triad|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in what became {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Honji Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}}, marking the origins of the Highada-monto tradition and the Shinshū Takada-ha sect.

The temple was officially named ''Senshū Amida-ji'' in [[:en:1226|1226]] by imperial decree, and Shinran spent about seven years there before returning to Kyoto. After Shinran's departure, his prominent disciple {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu|1=Shinbutsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6606696}} succeeded him as the second head priest of Senjuji, followed by {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenchi|1=Kenchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6605658}}.

=== Sengoku Period ===
During the 15th century, the sect expanded significantly under the leadership of the 10th head priest, {{ill|Shin'e|ja|真恵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin'e|1=Shin'e|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q85271929}}, who actively spread Jōdo Shinshū teachings across [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai region]] and [[:en:Hokuriku region|Hokuriku region]]. However, conflicts arose with {{ill|Rennyo|ja|蓮妃|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rennyo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the influential head of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honganji|1=Hongan-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11521048}}, leading many followers and temples to shift allegiance to the Honganji faction. In [[:en:1465|1465]], Shin'e petitioned {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比士山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Hiei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to recognize Takada-ha as separate from Honganji, which was granted.

Despite setbacks due to military conflicts, including the {{ill|Echizen Ikkō-Ikki|ja|越前一向一挑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echizen Ikkō-Ikki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Takada-ha retained a strong presence in certain regions. By the 16th century, the sect moved its primary temple from [[:en:Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke Province]] to {{ill|Isshinden|ja|一身田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isshinden|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]], where it remains today.

=== Edo Period ===
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Takada-ha maintained its status as one of the largest Jōdo Shinshū sects after the split of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honganji|1=Hongan-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11521048}} into the [[:en:Nishi Hongan-ji|Nishi Honganji]] and [[:en:Higashi Hongan-ji|Higashi Honganji]] branches. The sect continued to flourish, with its head temple, Senjuji, recognized as a ''jun-monzeki'' (quasi-imperial temple).

== Major Temples ==
; Head Temple
* {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Honji Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}} (Mooka, Tochigi Prefecture)

; Main Temple
* {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Honji Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}} (Isshinden, Tsu, Mie Prefecture)

; Branch Temples
* Kyoto Betsuin
* {{ill|Nagoya Betsuin|ja|專修寺名古屋別院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagoya Betsuin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* Kobe Betsuin
* Hokkaido Betsuin (Kiyota-ku, Sapporo, Hokkaido)
* Fukui Betsuin
* Kanto Betsuin
* Yokohama Betsuin

== Characteristics ==
Takada-ha uniquely uses ''Bunrui-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of the Pure Land|ja|文類慰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hymns of the Pure Land|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) for evening services, unlike other Jōdo Shinshū sects that primarily recite ''Shōshin-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of True Faith|ja|正信慰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hymns of True Faith|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). Additionally, its temples traditionally do not use {{ill|Tannishō|ja|商異止|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tannishō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, as the sect preserves a large collection of Shinran's original writings.

After Shinran's departure, his prominent disciple {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu|1=Shinbutsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6606696}} succeeded him as the second head priest of Senjuji, followed by {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenchi|1=Kenchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6605658}}.

=== Sengoku Period ===
During the 15th century, the sect expanded significantly under the leadership of the 10th head priest, {{ill|Shin'e|ja|真恵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin'e|1=Shin'e|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q85271929}}, who actively spread Jōdo Shinshū teachings across [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai region]] and [[:en:Hokuriku region|Hokuriku region]]. However, conflicts arose with {{ill|Rennyo|ja|蓮妃|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rennyo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the influential head of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honganji|1=Hongan-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11521048}}, leading many followers and temples to shift allegiance to the Honganji faction. In [[:en:1465|1465]], Shin'e petitioned {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比士山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Hiei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to recognize Takada-ha as separate from Honganji, which was granted.

Despite setbacks due to military conflicts, including the {{ill|Echizen Ikkō-Ikki|ja|越前一向一挑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echizen Ikkō-Ikki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Takada-ha retained a strong presence in certain regions. By the 16th century, the sect moved its primary temple from [[:en:Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke Province]] to {{ill|Isshinden|ja|一身田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isshinden|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]], where it remains today.

=== Edo Period ===
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Takada-ha maintained its status as one of the largest Jōdo Shinshū sects after the split of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honganji|1=Hongan-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11521048}} into the [[:en:Nishi Hongan-ji|Nishi Honganji]] and [[:en:Higashi Hongan-ji|Higashi Honganji]] branches. The sect continued to flourish, with its head temple, Senjuji, recognized as a ''jun-monzeki'' (quasi-imperial temple).

== Major Temples ==
; Head Temple
* {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Honji Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}} (Mooka, Tochigi Prefecture)

; Main Temple
* {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Honji Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}} (Isshinden, Tsu, Mie Prefecture)

; Branch Temples
* Kyoto Betsuin
* {{ill|Nagoya Betsuin|ja|專修寺名古屋別院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagoya Betsuin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* Kobe Betsuin
* Hokkaido Betsuin (Kiyota-ku, Sapporo, Hokkaido)
* Fukui Betsuin
* Kanto Betsuin
* Yokohama Betsuin

== Characteristics ==
Takada-ha uniquely uses ''Bunrui-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of the Pure Land|ja|文類慰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hymns of the Pure Land|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) for evening services, unlike other Jōdo Shinshū sects that primarily recite ''Shōshin-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of True Faith|ja|正信慰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hymns of True Faith|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). Additionally, its temples traditionally do not use {{ill|Tannishō|ja|商異止|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tannishō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, as the sect preserves a large collection of Shinran's original writings.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:真宗高田派]][[zh:真宗高田派]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q10437214]] Wikidata name: Shinshū Takada school [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox organization | name = Shinshū Takada-ha | native_name = 真宗高田派 | image = [[File:Shinshu Takada-ha Shumuin.JPG|250px]] | caption = The Religious Affairs Office at the head temple [[:en:Senjuji|Senjuji]] | type = [[Religious corporation]] | headquarters = 2819 Isshinden-chō, Tsu City, Mie Prefecture | leader_title = Hōshu (Head Priest) | leader_name = Jishō | website = {{URL|http://www.senjuji.or.jp/}} }}
'''Shinshū Takada-ha''' (真宗高田派) is a branch of {{ill|Jōdo Shinshū|ja|淡土真宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōdo Shinshū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, with its head temple at {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Honji Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}} in {{ill|Tsu, Mie|ja|渡哥市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsu, Mie|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It traces its origins to the disciples {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu|1=Shinbutsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6606696}} and {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenchi|1=Kenchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6605658}}, who led the Highada-monto community in the medieval period. The sect has approximately 640 affiliated temples.
== History ==
=== Founding Period === In [[:en:1205|1205]], during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Go-Toba]], the practice of {{ill|Senju Nembutsu|ja|専修念仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senju Nembutsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 称名念仏|1=chengming nianfo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11596185}}, propagated by [[:en:Hōnen|Hōnen]] and his disciples, was banned, leading to the exile of {{ill|Shinran|ja|親熊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinran|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to [[:en:Echigo Province|Echigo Province]]. After being pardoned in [[:en:1211|1211]], Shinran traveled to [[:en:Zenkō-ji|Zenkoji]] and then settled in {{ill|Hitachi Province|ja|常陽国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitachi Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where he began his missionary activities.
Shinran established his base at {{ill|Nishi Nembutsuji|ja|西念仏寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishi Nembutsuji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Hitachi, and in [[:en:1224|1224]], he completed his seminal work, {{ill|Kyōgyōshinshō|ja|顯淡土真実教行証文類|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōgyōshinshō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ('Teaching, Practice, Faith, and Attainment'), which laid the doctrinal foundation for Jōdo Shinshū.
In [[:en:1225|1225]], according to tradition, Shinran received a revelation instructing him to establish a temple in Highada. With the support of [[:en:Ōuchi clan|Ōuchi Clan]], the ruler of {{ill|Mooka Castle|ja|真岡城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mooka Castle|1=Mooka Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11583106}}, he enshrined a replica of the {{ill|Zenkoji-style Amida Triad|ja|善光寺式阿弥陀三宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenkoji-style Amida Triad|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in what became {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Honji Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}}, marking the origins of the Highada-monto tradition and the Shinshū Takada-ha sect.
The temple was officially named ''Senshū Amida-ji'' in [[:en:1226|1226]] by imperial decree, and Shinran spent about seven years there before returning to Kyoto. After Shinran's departure, his prominent disciple {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu|1=Shinbutsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6606696}} succeeded him as the second head priest of Senjuji, followed by {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenchi|1=Kenchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6605658}}.
=== Sengoku Period === During the 15th century, the sect expanded significantly under the leadership of the 10th head priest, {{ill|Shin'e|ja|真恵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin'e|1=Shin'e|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q85271929}}, who actively spread Jōdo Shinshū teachings across [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai region]] and [[:en:Hokuriku region|Hokuriku region]]. However, conflicts arose with {{ill|Rennyo|ja|蓮妃|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rennyo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the influential head of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honganji|1=Hongan-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11521048}}, leading many followers and temples to shift allegiance to the Honganji faction. In [[:en:1465|1465]], Shin'e petitioned {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比士山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Hiei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to recognize Takada-ha as separate from Honganji, which was granted.
Despite setbacks due to military conflicts, including the {{ill|Echizen Ikkō-Ikki|ja|越前一向一挑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echizen Ikkō-Ikki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Takada-ha retained a strong presence in certain regions. By the 16th century, the sect moved its primary temple from [[:en:Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke Province]] to {{ill|Isshinden|ja|一身田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isshinden|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]], where it remains today.
=== Edo Period === During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Takada-ha maintained its status as one of the largest Jōdo Shinshū sects after the split of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honganji|1=Hongan-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11521048}} into the [[:en:Nishi Hongan-ji|Nishi Honganji]] and [[:en:Higashi Hongan-ji|Higashi Honganji]] branches. The sect continued to flourish, with its head temple, Senjuji, recognized as a ''jun-monzeki'' (quasi-imperial temple).
== Major Temples == ; Head Temple * {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Honji Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}} (Mooka, Tochigi Prefecture)
; Main Temple * {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Honji Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}} (Isshinden, Tsu, Mie Prefecture)
; Branch Temples * Kyoto Betsuin * {{ill|Nagoya Betsuin|ja|專修寺名古屋別院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagoya Betsuin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * Kobe Betsuin * Hokkaido Betsuin (Kiyota-ku, Sapporo, Hokkaido) * Fukui Betsuin * Kanto Betsuin * Yokohama Betsuin
== Characteristics == Takada-ha uniquely uses ''Bunrui-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of the Pure Land|ja|文類慰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hymns of the Pure Land|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) for evening services, unlike other Jōdo Shinshū sects that primarily recite ''Shōshin-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of True Faith|ja|正信慰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hymns of True Faith|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). Additionally, its temples traditionally do not use {{ill|Tannishō|ja|商異止|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tannishō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, as the sect preserves a large collection of Shinran's original writings.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:真宗高田派]][[zh:真宗高田派]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q10437214]] Wikidata name: Shinshū Takada school [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox organization | name = Shinshū Takada-ha | native_name = 真宗高田派 | image = [[File:Shinshu Takada-ha Shumuin.JPG|250px]] | caption = The Religious Affairs Office at the head temple [[:en:Senjuji|Senjuji]] | type = [[Religious corporation]] | headquarters = 2819 Isshinden-chō, Tsu City, Mie Prefecture | leader_title = Hōshu (Head Priest) | leader_name = Jishō | website = {{URL|http://www.senjuji.or.jp/}} }}
'''Shinshū Takada-ha''' (真宗高田派) is a branch of {{ill|Jōdo Shinshū|ja|淡土真宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōdo Shinshū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, with its head temple at {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Honji Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}} in {{ill|Tsu, Mie|ja|渡哥市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsu, Mie|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It traces its origins to the disciples {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu|1=Shinbutsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6606696}} and {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenchi|1=Kenchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6605658}}, who led the Highada-monto community in the medieval period. The sect has approximately 640 affiliated temples.
== History ==
=== Founding Period === In [[:en:1205|1205]], during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Go-Toba]], the practice of {{ill|Senju Nembutsu|ja|専修念仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senju Nembutsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 称名念仏|1=chengming nianfo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11596185}}, propagated by [[:en:Hōnen|Hōnen]] and his disciples, was banned, leading to the exile of {{ill|Shinran|ja|親熊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinran|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to [[:en:Echigo Province|Echigo Province]]. After being pardoned in [[:en:1211|1211]], Shinran traveled to [[:en:Zenkō-ji|Zenkoji]] and then settled in {{ill|Hitachi Province|ja|常陽国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitachi Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where he began his missionary activities.
Shinran established his base at {{ill|Nishi Nembutsuji|ja|西念仏寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishi Nembutsuji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Hitachi, and in [[:en:1224|1224]], he completed his seminal work, {{ill|Kyōgyōshinshō|ja|顯淡土真実教行証文類|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōgyōshinshō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ('Teaching, Practice, Faith, and Attainment'), which laid the doctrinal foundation for Jōdo Shinshū.
In [[:en:1225|1225]], according to tradition, Shinran received a revelation instructing him to establish a temple in Highada. With the support of [[:en:Ōuchi clan|Ōuchi Clan]], the ruler of {{ill|Mooka Castle|ja|真岡城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mooka Castle|1=Mooka Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11583106}}, he enshrined a replica of the {{ill|Zenkoji-style Amida Triad|ja|善光寺式阿弥陀三宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenkoji-style Amida Triad|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in what became {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Honji Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}}, marking the origins of the Highada-monto tradition and the Shinshū Takada-ha sect.
The temple was officially named ''Senshū Amida-ji'' in [[:en:1226|1226]] by imperial decree, and Shinran spent about seven years there before returning to Kyoto. After Shinran's departure, his prominent disciple {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu|1=Shinbutsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6606696}} succeeded him as the second head priest of Senjuji, followed by {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenchi|1=Kenchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6605658}}.
=== Sengoku Period === During the 15th century, the sect expanded significantly under the leadership of the 10th head priest, {{ill|Shin'e|ja|真恵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin'e|1=Shin'e|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q85271929}}, who actively spread Jōdo Shinshū teachings across [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai region]] and [[:en:Hokuriku region|Hokuriku region]]. However, conflicts arose with {{ill|Rennyo|ja|蓮妃|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rennyo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the influential head of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honganji|1=Hongan-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11521048}}, leading many followers and temples to shift allegiance to the Honganji faction. In [[:en:1465|1465]], Shin'e petitioned {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比士山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Hiei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to recognize Takada-ha as separate from Honganji, which was granted.
Despite setbacks due to military conflicts, including the {{ill|Echizen Ikkō-Ikki|ja|越前一向一挑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echizen Ikkō-Ikki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Takada-ha retained a strong presence in certain regions. By the 16th century, the sect moved its primary temple from [[:en:Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke Province]] to {{ill|Isshinden|ja|一身田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isshinden|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]], where it remains today.
=== Edo Period === During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Takada-ha maintained its status as one of the largest Jōdo Shinshū sects after the split of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honganji|1=Hongan-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11521048}} into the [[:en:Nishi Hongan-ji|Nishi Honganji]] and [[:en:Higashi Hongan-ji|Higashi Honganji]] branches. The sect continued to flourish, with its head temple, Senjuji, recognized as a ''jun-monzeki'' (quasi-imperial temple).
== Major Temples == ; Head Temple * {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Honji Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}} (Mooka, Tochigi Prefecture)
; Main Temple * {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Honji Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}} (Isshinden, Tsu, Mie Prefecture)
; Branch Temples * Kyoto Betsuin * {{ill|Nagoya Betsuin|ja|專修寺名古屋別院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagoya Betsuin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * Kobe Betsuin * Hokkaido Betsuin (Kiyota-ku, Sapporo, Hokkaido) * Fukui Betsuin * Kanto Betsuin * Yokohama Betsuin
== Characteristics == Takada-ha uniquely uses ''Bunrui-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of the Pure Land|ja|文類慰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hymns of the Pure Land|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) for evening services, unlike other Jōdo Shinshū sects that primarily recite ''Shōshin-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of True Faith|ja|正信慰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hymns of True Faith|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). Additionally, its temples traditionally do not use {{ill|Tannishō|ja|商異止|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tannishō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, as the sect preserves a large collection of Shinran's original writings.

'''Shinshū Takada-ha''' (真宗高田派) is a branch of {{ill|Jōdo Shinshū|ja|淡土真宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōdo Shinshū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, with its head temple at {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Honji Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}} in {{ill|Tsu, Mie|ja|渡哥市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsu, Mie|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It traces its origins to the disciples {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu|1=Shinbutsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6606696}} and {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenchi|1=Kenchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6605658}}, who led the Highada-monto community in the medieval period. The sect has approximately 640 affiliated temples.
== History ==
=== Founding Period === In [[:en:1205|1205]], during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Go-Toba]], the practice of {{ill|Senju Nembutsu|ja|専修念仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senju Nembutsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 称名念仏|1=chengming nianfo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11596185}}, propagated by [[:en:Hōnen|Hōnen]] and his disciples, was banned, leading to the exile of {{ill|Shinran|ja|親熊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinran|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to [[:en:Echigo Province|Echigo Province]]. After being pardoned in [[:en:1211|1211]], Shinran traveled to [[:en:Zenkō-ji|Zenkoji]] and then settled in {{ill|Hitachi Province|ja|常陽国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitachi Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where he began his missionary activities.
Shinran established his base at {{ill|Nishi Nembutsuji|ja|西念仏寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishi Nembutsuji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Hitachi, and in [[:en:1224|1224]], he completed his seminal work, {{ill|Kyōgyōshinshō|ja|顯淡土真実教行証文類|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōgyōshinshō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ('Teaching, Practice, Faith, and Attainment'), which laid the doctrinal foundation for Jōdo Shinshū.
In [[:en:1225|1225]], according to tradition, Shinran received a revelation instructing him to establish a temple in Highada. With the support of [[:en:Ōuchi clan|Ōuchi Clan]], the ruler of {{ill|Mooka Castle|ja|真岡城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mooka Castle|1=Mooka Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11583106}}, he enshrined a replica of the {{ill|Zenkoji-style Amida Triad|ja|善光寺式阿弥陀三宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenkoji-style Amida Triad|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in what became {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Honji Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}}, marking the origins of the Highada-monto tradition and the Shinshū Takada-ha sect.
The temple was officially named ''Senshū Amida-ji'' in [[:en:1226|1226]] by imperial decree, and Shinran spent about seven years there before returning to Kyoto. After Shinran's departure, his prominent disciple {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu|1=Shinbutsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6606696}} succeeded him as the second head priest of Senjuji, followed by {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenchi|1=Kenchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6605658}}.
=== Sengoku Period === During the 15th century, the sect expanded significantly under the leadership of the 10th head priest, {{ill|Shin'e|ja|真恵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin'e|1=Shin'e|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q85271929}}, who actively spread Jōdo Shinshū teachings across [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai region]] and [[:en:Hokuriku region|Hokuriku region]]. However, conflicts arose with {{ill|Rennyo|ja|蓮妃|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rennyo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the influential head of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honganji|1=Hongan-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11521048}}, leading many followers and temples to shift allegiance to the Honganji faction. In [[:en:1465|1465]], Shin'e petitioned {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比士山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Hiei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to recognize Takada-ha as separate from Honganji, which was granted.
Despite setbacks due to military conflicts, including the {{ill|Echizen Ikkō-Ikki|ja|越前一向一挑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echizen Ikkō-Ikki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Takada-ha retained a strong presence in certain regions. By the 16th century, the sect moved its primary temple from [[:en:Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke Province]] to {{ill|Isshinden|ja|一身田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isshinden|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]], where it remains today.
=== Edo Period === During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Takada-ha maintained its status as one of the largest Jōdo Shinshū sects after the split of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honganji|1=Hongan-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11521048}} into the [[:en:Nishi Hongan-ji|Nishi Honganji]] and [[:en:Higashi Hongan-ji|Higashi Honganji]] branches. The sect continued to flourish, with its head temple, Senjuji, recognized as a ''jun-monzeki'' (quasi-imperial temple).
== Major Temples == ; Head Temple * {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Honji Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}} (Mooka, Tochigi Prefecture)
; Main Temple * {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Honji Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}} (Isshinden, Tsu, Mie Prefecture)
; Branch Temples * Kyoto Betsuin * {{ill|Nagoya Betsuin|ja|專修寺名古屋別院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagoya Betsuin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * Kobe Betsuin * Hokkaido Betsuin (Kiyota-ku, Sapporo, Hokkaido) * Fukui Betsuin * Kanto Betsuin * Yokohama Betsuin
== Characteristics == Takada-ha uniquely uses ''Bunrui-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of the Pure Land|ja|文類慰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hymns of the Pure Land|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) for evening services, unlike other Jōdo Shinshū sects that primarily recite ''Shōshin-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of True Faith|ja|正信慰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hymns of True Faith|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). Additionally, its temples traditionally do not use {{ill|Tannishō|ja|商異止|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tannishō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, as the sect preserves a large collection of Shinran's original writings.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:真宗高田派]][[zh:真宗高田派]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q10437214]] Wikidata name: Shinshū Takada school [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox organization | name = Shinshū Takada-ha | native_name = 真宗高田派 | image = [[File:Shinshu Takada-ha Shumuin.JPG|250px]] | caption = The Religious Affairs Office at the head temple [[:en:Senjuji|Senjuji]] | type = [[Religious corporation]] | headquarters = 2819 Isshinden-chō, Tsu City, Mie Prefecture | leader_title = Hōshu (Head Priest) | leader_name = Jishō | website = {{URL|http://www.senjuji.or.jp/}} }}
'''Shinshū Takada-ha''' (真宗高田派) is a branch of {{ill|Jōdo Shinshū|ja|淡土真宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōdo Shinshū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, with its head temple at {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Honji Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}} in {{ill|Tsu, Mie|ja|渡哥市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsu, Mie|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It traces its origins to the disciples {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu|1=Shinbutsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6606696}} and {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenchi|1=Kenchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6605658}}, who led the Highada-monto community in the medieval period. The sect has approximately 640 affiliated temples.
== History ==
=== Founding Period === In [[:en:1205|1205]], during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Go-Toba]], the practice of {{ill|Senju Nembutsu|ja|専修念仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senju Nembutsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 称名念仏|1=chengming nianfo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11596185}}, propagated by [[:en:Hōnen|Hōnen]] and his disciples, was banned, leading to the exile of {{ill|Shinran|ja|親熊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinran|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to [[:en:Echigo Province|Echigo Province]]. After being pardoned in [[:en:1211|1211]], Shinran traveled to [[:en:Zenkō-ji|Zenkoji]] and then settled in {{ill|Hitachi Province|ja|常陽国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitachi Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where he began his missionary activities.
Shinran established his base at {{ill|Nishi Nembutsuji|ja|西念仏寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishi Nembutsuji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Hitachi, and in [[:en:1224|1224]], he completed his seminal work, {{ill|Kyōgyōshinshō|ja|顯淡土真実教行証文類|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōgyōshinshō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ('Teaching, Practice, Faith, and Attainment'), which laid the doctrinal foundation for Jōdo Shinshū.
In [[:en:1225|1225]], according to tradition, Shinran received a revelation instructing him to establish a temple in Highada. With the support of [[:en:Ōuchi clan|Ōuchi Clan]], the ruler of {{ill|Mooka Castle|ja|真岡城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mooka Castle|1=Mooka Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11583106}}, he enshrined a replica of the {{ill|Zenkoji-style Amida Triad|ja|善光寺式阿弥陀三宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenkoji-style Amida Triad|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in what became {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Honji Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}}, marking the origins of the Highada-monto tradition and the Shinshū Takada-ha sect.
The temple was officially named ''Senshū Amida-ji'' in [[:en:1226|1226]] by imperial decree, and Shinran spent about seven years there before returning to Kyoto. After Shinran's departure, his prominent disciple {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu|1=Shinbutsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6606696}} succeeded him as the second head priest of Senjuji, followed by {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenchi|1=Kenchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6605658}}.
=== Sengoku Period === During the 15th century, the sect expanded significantly under the leadership of the 10th head priest, {{ill|Shin'e|ja|真恵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin'e|1=Shin'e|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q85271929}}, who actively spread Jōdo Shinshū teachings across [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai region]] and [[:en:Hokuriku region|Hokuriku region]]. However, conflicts arose with {{ill|Rennyo|ja|蓮妃|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rennyo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the influential head of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honganji|1=Hongan-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11521048}}, leading many followers and temples to shift allegiance to the Honganji faction. In [[:en:1465|1465]], Shin'e petitioned {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比士山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Hiei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to recognize Takada-ha as separate from Honganji, which was granted.
Despite setbacks due to military conflicts, including the {{ill|Echizen Ikkō-Ikki|ja|越前一向一挑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echizen Ikkō-Ikki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Takada-ha retained a strong presence in certain regions. By the 16th century, the sect moved its primary temple from [[:en:Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke Province]] to {{ill|Isshinden|ja|一身田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isshinden|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]], where it remains today.
=== Edo Period === During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Takada-ha maintained its status as one of the largest Jōdo Shinshū sects after the split of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honganji|1=Hongan-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11521048}} into the [[:en:Nishi Hongan-ji|Nishi Honganji]] and [[:en:Higashi Hongan-ji|Higashi Honganji]] branches. The sect continued to flourish, with its head temple, Senjuji, recognized as a ''jun-monzeki'' (quasi-imperial temple).
== Major Temples == ; Head Temple * {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Honji Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}} (Mooka, Tochigi Prefecture)
; Main Temple * {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Honji Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}} (Isshinden, Tsu, Mie Prefecture)
; Branch Temples * Kyoto Betsuin * {{ill|Nagoya Betsuin|ja|專修寺名古屋別院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagoya Betsuin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * Kobe Betsuin * Hokkaido Betsuin (Kiyota-ku, Sapporo, Hokkaido) * Fukui Betsuin * Kanto Betsuin * Yokohama Betsuin
== Characteristics == Takada-ha uniquely uses ''Bunrui-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of the Pure Land|ja|文類慰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hymns of the Pure Land|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) for evening services, unlike other Jōdo Shinshū sects that primarily recite ''Shōshin-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of True Faith|ja|正信慰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hymns of True Faith|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). Additionally, its temples traditionally do not use {{ill|Tannishō|ja|商異止|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tannishō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, as the sect preserves a large collection of Shinran's original writings.

{{Infobox organization
| name = Shinshū Takada-ha
| native_name = 真宗高田派
| image = [[File:Shinshu Takada-ha Shumuin.JPG|250px]]
| caption = The Religious Affairs Office at the head temple [[:en:Senjuji|Senjuji]]
| type = [[Religious corporation]]
| headquarters = 2819 Isshinden-chō, Tsu City, Mie Prefecture
| leader_title = Hōshu (Head Priest)
| leader_name = Jishō
| website = {{URL|http://www.senjuji.or.jp/}}
}}

'''Shinshū Takada-ha''' (真宗高田派) is a branch of {{ill|Jōdo Shinshū|ja|淡土真宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōdo Shinshū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, with its head temple at {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Honji Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}} in {{ill|Tsu, Mie|ja|渡哥市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsu, Mie|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It traces its origins to the disciples {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu|1=Shinbutsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6606696}} and {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenchi|1=Kenchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6605658}}, who led the Highada-monto community in the medieval period. The sect has approximately 640 affiliated temples.

== History ==

=== Founding Period ===
In [[:en:1205|1205]], during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Go-Toba]], the practice of {{ill|Senju Nembutsu|ja|専修念仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senju Nembutsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 称名念仏|1=chengming nianfo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11596185}}, propagated by [[:en:Hōnen|Hōnen]] and his disciples, was banned, leading to the exile of {{ill|Shinran|ja|親熊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinran|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to [[:en:Echigo Province|Echigo Province]]. After being pardoned in [[:en:1211|1211]], Shinran traveled to [[:en:Zenkō-ji|Zenkoji]] and then settled in {{ill|Hitachi Province|ja|常陽国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitachi Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where he began his missionary activities.

Shinran established his base at {{ill|Nishi Nembutsuji|ja|西念仏寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishi Nembutsuji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Hitachi, and in [[:en:1224|1224]], he completed his seminal work, {{ill|Kyōgyōshinshō|ja|顯淡土真実教行証文類|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōgyōshinshō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ('Teaching, Practice, Faith, and Attainment'), which laid the doctrinal foundation for Jōdo Shinshū.

In [[:en:1225|1225]], according to tradition, Shinran received a revelation instructing him to establish a temple in Highada. With the support of [[:en:Ōuchi clan|Ōuchi Clan]], the ruler of {{ill|Mooka Castle|ja|真岡城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mooka Castle|1=Mooka Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11583106}}, he enshrined a replica of the {{ill|Zenkoji-style Amida Triad|ja|善光寺式阿弥陀三宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenkoji-style Amida Triad|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in what became {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Honji Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}}, marking the origins of the Highada-monto tradition and the Shinshū Takada-ha sect.

The temple was officially named ''Senshū Amida-ji'' in [[:en:1226|1226]] by imperial decree, and Shinran spent about seven years there before returning to Kyoto. After Shinran's departure, his prominent disciple {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu|1=Shinbutsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6606696}} succeeded him as the second head priest of Senjuji, followed by {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenchi|1=Kenchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6605658}}.

=== Sengoku Period ===
During the 15th century, the sect expanded significantly under the leadership of the 10th head priest, {{ill|Shin'e|ja|真恵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin'e|1=Shin'e|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q85271929}}, who actively spread Jōdo Shinshū teachings across [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai region]] and [[:en:Hokuriku region|Hokuriku region]]. However, conflicts arose with {{ill|Rennyo|ja|蓮妃|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rennyo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the influential head of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honganji|1=Hongan-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11521048}}, leading many followers and temples to shift allegiance to the Honganji faction. In [[:en:1465|1465]], Shin'e petitioned {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比士山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Hiei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to recognize Takada-ha as separate from Honganji, which was granted.

Despite setbacks due to military conflicts, including the {{ill|Echizen Ikkō-Ikki|ja|越前一向一挑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echizen Ikkō-Ikki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Takada-ha retained a strong presence in certain regions. By the 16th century, the sect moved its primary temple from [[:en:Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke Province]] to {{ill|Isshinden|ja|一身田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isshinden|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]], where it remains today.

=== Edo Period ===
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Takada-ha maintained its status as one of the largest Jōdo Shinshū sects after the split of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honganji|1=Hongan-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11521048}} into the [[:en:Nishi Hongan-ji|Nishi Honganji]] and [[:en:Higashi Hongan-ji|Higashi Honganji]] branches. The sect continued to flourish, with its head temple, Senjuji, recognized as a ''jun-monzeki'' (quasi-imperial temple).

== Major Temples ==
; Head Temple
* {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Honji Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}} (Mooka, Tochigi Prefecture)

; Main Temple
* {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Honji Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}} (Isshinden, Tsu, Mie Prefecture)

; Branch Temples
* Kyoto Betsuin
* {{ill|Nagoya Betsuin|ja|專修寺名古屋別院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagoya Betsuin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* Kobe Betsuin
* Hokkaido Betsuin (Kiyota-ku, Sapporo, Hokkaido)
* Fukui Betsuin
* Kanto Betsuin
* Yokohama Betsuin

== Characteristics ==
Takada-ha uniquely uses ''Bunrui-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of the Pure Land|ja|文類慰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hymns of the Pure Land|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) for evening services, unlike other Jōdo Shinshū sects that primarily recite ''Shōshin-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of True Faith|ja|正信慰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hymns of True Faith|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). Additionally, its temples traditionally do not use {{ill|Tannishō|ja|商異止|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tannishō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, as the sect preserves a large collection of Shinran's original writings.

With the support of [[:en:Ōuchi clan|Ōuchi Clan]], the ruler of {{ill|Mooka Castle|ja|真岡城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mooka Castle|1=Mooka Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11583106}}, he enshrined a replica of the {{ill|Zenkoji-style Amida Triad|ja|善光寺式阿弥陀三宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenkoji-style Amida Triad|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in what became {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Honji Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}}, marking the origins of the Highada-monto tradition and the Shinshū Takada-ha sect.

The temple was officially named ''Senshū Amida-ji'' in [[:en:1226|1226]] by imperial decree, and Shinran spent about seven years there before returning to Kyoto. After Shinran's departure, his prominent disciple {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu|1=Shinbutsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6606696}} succeeded him as the second head priest of Senjuji, followed by {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenchi|1=Kenchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6605658}}.

=== Sengoku Period ===
During the 15th century, the sect expanded significantly under the leadership of the 10th head priest, {{ill|Shin'e|ja|真恵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin'e|1=Shin'e|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q85271929}}, who actively spread Jōdo Shinshū teachings across [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai region]] and [[:en:Hokuriku region|Hokuriku region]]. However, conflicts arose with {{ill|Rennyo|ja|蓮妃|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rennyo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the influential head of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honganji|1=Hongan-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11521048}}, leading many followers and temples to shift allegiance to the Honganji faction. In [[:en:1465|1465]], Shin'e petitioned {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比士山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Hiei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to recognize Takada-ha as separate from Honganji, which was granted.

Despite setbacks due to military conflicts, including the {{ill|Echizen Ikkō-Ikki|ja|越前一向一挑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echizen Ikkō-Ikki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Takada-ha retained a strong presence in certain regions. By the 16th century, the sect moved its primary temple from [[:en:Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke Province]] to {{ill|Isshinden|ja|一身田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isshinden|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]], where it remains today.

=== Edo Period ===
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Takada-ha maintained its status as one of the largest Jōdo Shinshū sects after the split of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honganji|1=Hongan-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11521048}} into the [[:en:Nishi Hongan-ji|Nishi Honganji]] and [[:en:Higashi Hongan-ji|Higashi Honganji]] branches. The sect continued to flourish, with its head temple, Senjuji, recognized as a ''jun-monzeki'' (quasi-imperial temple).

== Major Temples ==
; Head Temple
* {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Honji Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}} (Mooka, Tochigi Prefecture)

; Main Temple
* {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Honji Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}} (Isshinden, Tsu, Mie Prefecture)

; Branch Temples
* Kyoto Betsuin
* {{ill|Nagoya Betsuin|ja|專修寺名古屋別院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagoya Betsuin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* Kobe Betsuin
* Hokkaido Betsuin (Kiyota-ku, Sapporo, Hokkaido)
* Fukui Betsuin
* Kanto Betsuin
* Yokohama Betsuin

== Characteristics ==
Takada-ha uniquely uses ''Bunrui-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of the Pure Land|ja|文類慰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hymns of the Pure Land|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) for evening services, unlike other Jōdo Shinshū sects that primarily recite ''Shōshin-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of True Faith|ja|正信慰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hymns of True Faith|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). Additionally, its temples traditionally do not use {{ill|Tannishō|ja|商異止|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tannishō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, as the sect preserves a large collection of Shinran's original writings.

'''Shinshū Takada-ha''' (真宗高田派) is a branch of {{ill|Jōdo Shinshū|ja|淡土真宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōdo Shinshū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, with its head temple at {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Honji Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}} in {{ill|Tsu, Mie|ja|渡哥市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsu, Mie|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It traces its origins to the disciples {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu|1=Shinbutsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6606696}} and {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenchi|1=Kenchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6605658}}, who led the Highada-monto community in the medieval period. The sect has approximately 640 affiliated temples.

== History ==

=== Founding Period ===
In [[:en:1205|1205]], during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Go-Toba]], the practice of {{ill|Senju Nembutsu|ja|専修念仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senju Nembutsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 称名念仏|1=chengming nianfo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11596185}}, propagated by [[:en:Hōnen|Hōnen]] and his disciples, was banned, leading to the exile of {{ill|Shinran|ja|親熊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinran|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to [[:en:Echigo Province|Echigo Province]]. After being pardoned in [[:en:1211|1211]], Shinran traveled to [[:en:Zenkō-ji|Zenkoji]] and then settled in {{ill|Hitachi Province|ja|常陽国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitachi Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where he began his missionary activities.

Shinran established his base at {{ill|Nishi Nembutsuji|ja|西念仏寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishi Nembutsuji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Hitachi, and in [[:en:1224|1224]], he completed his seminal work, {{ill|Kyōgyōshinshō|ja|顯淡土真実教行証文類|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōgyōshinshō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ('Teaching, Practice, Faith, and Attainment'), which laid the doctrinal foundation for Jōdo Shinshū.

In [[:en:1225|1225]], according to tradition, Shinran received a revelation instructing him to establish a temple in Highada. With the support of [[:en:Ōuchi clan|Ōuchi Clan]], the ruler of {{ill|Mooka Castle|ja|真岡城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mooka Castle|1=Mooka Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11583106}}, he enshrined a replica of the {{ill|Zenkoji-style Amida Triad|ja|善光寺式阿弥陀三宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenkoji-style Amida Triad|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in what became {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Honji Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}}, marking the origins of the Highada-monto tradition and the Shinshū Takada-ha sect.

The temple was officially named ''Senshū Amida-ji'' in [[:en:1226|1226]] by imperial decree, and Shinran spent about seven years there before returning to Kyoto. After Shinran's departure, his prominent disciple {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu|1=Shinbutsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6606696}} succeeded him as the second head priest of Senjuji, followed by {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenchi|1=Kenchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6605658}}.

=== Sengoku Period ===
During the 15th century, the sect expanded significantly under the leadership of the 10th head priest, {{ill|Shin'e|ja|真恵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin'e|1=Shin'e|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q85271929}}, who actively spread Jōdo Shinshū teachings across [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai region]] and [[:en:Hokuriku region|Hokuriku region]]. However, conflicts arose with {{ill|Rennyo|ja|蓮妃|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rennyo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the influential head of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honganji|1=Hongan-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11521048}}, leading many followers and temples to shift allegiance to the Honganji faction. In [[:en:1465|1465]], Shin'e petitioned {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比士山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Hiei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to recognize Takada-ha as separate from Honganji, which was granted.

Despite setbacks due to military conflicts, including the {{ill|Echizen Ikkō-Ikki|ja|越前一向一挑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echizen Ikkō-Ikki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Takada-ha retained a strong presence in certain regions. By the 16th century, the sect moved its primary temple from [[:en:Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke Province]] to {{ill|Isshinden|ja|一身田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isshinden|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]], where it remains today.

=== Edo Period ===
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Takada-ha maintained its status as one of the largest Jōdo Shinshū sects after the split of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honganji|1=Hongan-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11521048}} into the [[:en:Nishi Hongan-ji|Nishi Honganji]] and [[:en:Higashi Hongan-ji|Higashi Honganji]] branches. The sect continued to flourish, with its head temple, Senjuji, recognized as a ''jun-monzeki'' (quasi-imperial temple).

Additionally, its temples traditionally do not use {{ill|Tannishō|ja|商異止|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tannishō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, as the sect preserves a large collection of Shinran's original writings.

. *The Medieval Religious Powers: Migration and Connection.* Chikuma Shinsho, 2008.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:真宗高田派]][[zh:真宗高田派]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:真宗高田派]][[zh:真宗高田派]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:真宗高田派]][[zh:真宗高田派]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q10437214]] Wikidata name: Shinshū Takada school [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox organization | name = Shinshū Takada-ha | native_name = 真宗高田派 | image = [[File:Shinshu Takada-ha Shumuin.JPG|250px]] | caption = The Religious Affairs Office at the head temple [[:en:Senjuji|Senjuji]] | type = [[Religious corporation]] | headquarters = 2819 Isshinden-chō, Tsu City, Mie Prefecture | leader_title = Hōshu (Head Priest) | leader_name = Jishō | website = {{URL|http://www.senjuji.or.jp/}} }}
'''Shinshū Takada-ha''' (真宗高田派) is a branch of {{ill|Jōdo Shinshū|ja|淡土真宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōdo Shinshū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, with its head temple at {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Honji Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}} in {{ill|Tsu, Mie|ja|渡哥市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsu, Mie|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It traces its origins to the disciples {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu|1=Shinbutsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6606696}} and {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenchi|1=Kenchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6605658}}, who led the Highada-monto community in the medieval period. The sect has approximately 640 affiliated temples.
== History ==
=== Founding Period === In [[:en:1205|1205]], during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Go-Toba]], the practice of {{ill|Senju Nembutsu|ja|専修念仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senju Nembutsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 称名念仏|1=chengming nianfo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11596185}}, propagated by [[:en:Hōnen|Hōnen]] and his disciples, was banned, leading to the exile of {{ill|Shinran|ja|親熊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinran|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to [[:en:Echigo Province|Echigo Province]]. After being pardoned in [[:en:1211|1211]], Shinran traveled to [[:en:Zenkō-ji|Zenkoji]] and then settled in {{ill|Hitachi Province|ja|常陽国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitachi Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where he began his missionary activities.
Shinran established his base at {{ill|Nishi Nembutsuji|ja|西念仏寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishi Nembutsuji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Hitachi, and in [[:en:1224|1224]], he completed his seminal work, {{ill|Kyōgyōshinshō|ja|顯淡土真実教行証文類|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōgyōshinshō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ('Teaching, Practice, Faith, and Attainment'), which laid the doctrinal foundation for Jōdo Shinshū.
In [[:en:1225|1225]], according to tradition, Shinran received a revelation instructing him to establish a temple in Highada. With the support of [[:en:Ōuchi clan|Ōuchi Clan]], the ruler of {{ill|Mooka Castle|ja|真岡城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mooka Castle|1=Mooka Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11583106}}, he enshrined a replica of the {{ill|Zenkoji-style Amida Triad|ja|善光寺式阿弥陀三宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenkoji-style Amida Triad|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in what became {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Honji Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}}, marking the origins of the Highada-monto tradition and the Shinshū Takada-ha sect.
The temple was officially named ''Senshū Amida-ji'' in [[:en:1226|1226]] by imperial decree, and Shinran spent about seven years there before returning to Kyoto. After Shinran's departure, his prominent disciple {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu|1=Shinbutsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6606696}} succeeded him as the second head priest of Senjuji, followed by {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenchi|1=Kenchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6605658}}.
=== Sengoku Period === During the 15th century, the sect expanded significantly under the leadership of the 10th head priest, {{ill|Shin'e|ja|真恵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin'e|1=Shin'e|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q85271929}}, who actively spread Jōdo Shinshū teachings across [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai region]] and [[:en:Hokuriku region|Hokuriku region]]. However, conflicts arose with {{ill|Rennyo|ja|蓮妃|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rennyo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the influential head of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honganji|1=Hongan-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11521048}}, leading many followers and temples to shift allegiance to the Honganji faction. In [[:en:1465|1465]], Shin'e petitioned {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比士山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Hiei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to recognize Takada-ha as separate from Honganji, which was granted.
Despite setbacks due to military conflicts, including the {{ill|Echizen Ikkō-Ikki|ja|越前一向一挑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echizen Ikkō-Ikki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Takada-ha retained a strong presence in certain regions. By the 16th century, the sect moved its primary temple from [[:en:Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke Province]] to {{ill|Isshinden|ja|一身田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isshinden|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]], where it remains today.
=== Edo Period === During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Takada-ha maintained its status as one of the largest Jōdo Shinshū sects after the split of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honganji|1=Hongan-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11521048}} into the [[:en:Nishi Hongan-ji|Nishi Honganji]] and [[:en:Higashi Hongan-ji|Higashi Honganji]] branches. The sect continued to flourish, with its head temple, Senjuji, recognized as a ''jun-monzeki'' (quasi-imperial temple).
== Major Temples == ; Head Temple * {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Honji Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}} (Mooka, Tochigi Prefecture)
; Main Temple * {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Honji Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}} (Isshinden, Tsu, Mie Prefecture)
; Branch Temples * Kyoto Betsuin * {{ill|Nagoya Betsuin|ja|專修寺名古屋別院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagoya Betsuin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * Kobe Betsuin * Hokkaido Betsuin (Kiyota-ku, Sapporo, Hokkaido) * Fukui Betsuin * Kanto Betsuin * Yokohama Betsuin
== Characteristics == Takada-ha uniquely uses ''Bunrui-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of the Pure Land|ja|文類慰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hymns of the Pure Land|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) for evening services, unlike other Jōdo Shinshū sects that primarily recite ''Shōshin-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of True Faith|ja|正信慰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hymns of True Faith|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). Additionally, its temples traditionally do not use {{ill|Tannishō|ja|商異止|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tannishō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, as the sect preserves a large collection of Shinran's original writings.

'''Shinshū Takada-ha''' (真宗高田派) is a branch of {{ill|Jōdo Shinshū|ja|淡土真宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōdo Shinshū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, with its head temple at {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Honji Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}} in {{ill|Tsu, Mie|ja|渡哥市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsu, Mie|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It traces its origins to the disciples {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu|1=Shinbutsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6606696}} and {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenchi|1=Kenchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6605658}}, who led the Highada-monto community in the medieval period. The sect has approximately 640 affiliated temples.
== History ==
=== Founding Period === In [[:en:1205|1205]], during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Go-Toba]], the practice of {{ill|Senju Nembutsu|ja|専修念仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senju Nembutsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 称名念仏|1=chengming nianfo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11596185}}, propagated by [[:en:Hōnen|Hōnen]] and his disciples, was banned, leading to the exile of {{ill|Shinran|ja|親熊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinran|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to [[:en:Echigo Province|Echigo Province]]. After being pardoned in [[:en:1211|1211]], Shinran traveled to [[:en:Zenkō-ji|Zenkoji]] and then settled in {{ill|Hitachi Province|ja|常陽国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitachi Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where he began his missionary activities.
Shinran established his base at {{ill|Nishi Nembutsuji|ja|西念仏寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishi Nembutsuji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Hitachi, and in [[:en:1224|1224]], he completed his seminal work, {{ill|Kyōgyōshinshō|ja|顯淡土真実教行証文類|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōgyōshinshō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ('Teaching, Practice, Faith, and Attainment'), which laid the doctrinal foundation for Jōdo Shinshū.
In [[:en:1225|1225]], according to tradition, Shinran received a revelation instructing him to establish a temple in Highada. With the support of [[:en:Ōuchi clan|Ōuchi Clan]], the ruler of {{ill|Mooka Castle|ja|真岡城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mooka Castle|1=Mooka Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11583106}}, he enshrined a replica of the {{ill|Zenkoji-style Amida Triad|ja|善光寺式阿弥陀三宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenkoji-style Amida Triad|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in what became {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Honji Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}}, marking the origins of the Highada-monto tradition and the Shinshū Takada-ha sect.
The temple was officially named ''Senshū Amida-ji'' in [[:en:1226|1226]] by imperial decree, and Shinran spent about seven years there before returning to Kyoto. After Shinran's departure, his prominent disciple {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu|1=Shinbutsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6606696}} succeeded him as the second head priest of Senjuji, followed by {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenchi|1=Kenchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6605658}}.
=== Sengoku Period === During the 15th century, the sect expanded significantly under the leadership of the 10th head priest, {{ill|Shin'e|ja|真恵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin'e|1=Shin'e|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q85271929}}, who actively spread Jōdo Shinshū teachings across [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai region]] and [[:en:Hokuriku region|Hokuriku region]]. However, conflicts arose with {{ill|Rennyo|ja|蓮妃|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rennyo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the influential head of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honganji|1=Hongan-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11521048}}, leading many followers and temples to shift allegiance to the Honganji faction. In [[:en:1465|1465]], Shin'e petitioned {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比士山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Hiei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to recognize Takada-ha as separate from Honganji, which was granted.
Despite setbacks due to military conflicts, including the {{ill|Echizen Ikkō-Ikki|ja|越前一向一挑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echizen Ikkō-Ikki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Takada-ha retained a strong presence in certain regions. By the 16th century, the sect moved its primary temple from [[:en:Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke Province]] to {{ill|Isshinden|ja|一身田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isshinden|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]], where it remains today.
=== Edo Period === During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Takada-ha maintained its status as one of the largest Jōdo Shinshū sects after the split of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honganji|1=Hongan-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11521048}} into the [[:en:Nishi Hongan-ji|Nishi Honganji]] and [[:en:Higashi Hongan-ji|Higashi Honganji]] branches. The sect continued to flourish, with its head temple, Senjuji, recognized as a ''jun-monzeki'' (quasi-imperial temple).
== Major Temples == ; Head Temple * {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Honji Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}} (Mooka, Tochigi Prefecture)
; Main Temple * {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Honji Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}} (Isshinden, Tsu, Mie Prefecture)
; Branch Temples * Kyoto Betsuin * {{ill|Nagoya Betsuin|ja|專修寺名古屋別院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagoya Betsuin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * Kobe Betsuin * Hokkaido Betsuin (Kiyota-ku, Sapporo, Hokkaido) * Fukui Betsuin * Kanto Betsuin * Yokohama Betsuin
== Characteristics == Takada-ha uniquely uses ''Bunrui-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of the Pure Land|ja|文類慰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hymns of the Pure Land|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) for evening services, unlike other Jōdo Shinshū sects that primarily recite ''Shōshin-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of True Faith|ja|正信慰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hymns of True Faith|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). Additionally, its temples traditionally do not use {{ill|Tannishō|ja|商異止|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tannishō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, as the sect preserves a large collection of Shinran's original writings.
