'''Shinshū Takada-ha''' (真宗高田派) is a branch of {{ill|Jōdo Shinshū|ja|淡土真宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōdo Shinshū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, with its head temple at {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Honji Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}} in {{ill|Tsu, Mie|ja|渡哥市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsu, Mie|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It traces its origins to the disciples {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu|1=Shinbutsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6606696}} and {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenchi|1=Kenchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6605658}}, who led the Highada-monto community in the medieval period. The sect has approximately 640 affiliated temples.
== History ==
=== Founding Period === In {{ill|1205|en|1205|WD=Q5229|lt=1205}}, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|en|Emperor Go-Toba|WD=Q316658|lt=Emperor Go-Toba}}, the practice of {{ill|Senju Nembutsu|ja|専修念仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senju Nembutsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 称名念仏|1=chengming nianfo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11596185}}, propagated by {{ill|Hōnen|en|Hōnen|WD=Q507980|lt=Hōnen}} and his disciples, was banned, leading to the exile of {{ill|Shinran|ja|親熊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinran|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to {{ill|Echigo Province|en|Echigo Province|WD=Q666514|lt=Echigo Province}}. After being pardoned in {{ill|1211|en|1211|WD=Q5251|lt=1211}}, Shinran traveled to {{ill|Zenkō-ji|en|Zenkō-ji|WD=Q189640|lt=Zenkoji}} and then settled in {{ill|Hitachi Province|ja|常陽国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitachi Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where he began his missionary activities.
Shinran established his base at {{ill|Nishi Nembutsuji|ja|西念仏寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishi Nembutsuji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Hitachi, and in {{ill|1224|en|1224|WD=Q5402|lt=1224}}, he completed his seminal work, {{ill|Kyōgyōshinshō|ja|顯淡土真実教行証文類|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōgyōshinshō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ('Teaching, Practice, Faith, and Attainment'), which laid the doctrinal foundation for Jōdo Shinshū.
In {{ill|1225|en|1225|WD=Q5403|lt=1225}}, according to tradition, Shinran received a revelation instructing him to establish a temple in Highada. With the support of {{ill|Ōuchi clan|en|Ōuchi clan|WD=Q1348909|lt=Ōuchi Clan}}, the ruler of {{ill|Mooka Castle|ja|真岡城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mooka Castle|1=Mooka Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11583106}}, he enshrined a replica of the {{ill|Zenkoji-style Amida Triad|ja|善光寺式阿弥陀三宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenkoji-style Amida Triad|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in what became {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Honji Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}}, marking the origins of the Highada-monto tradition and the Shinshū Takada-ha sect.
The temple was officially named ''Senshū Amida-ji'' in {{ill|1226|en|1226|WD=Q5405|lt=1226}} by imperial decree, and Shinran spent about seven years there before returning to Kyoto. After Shinran's departure, his prominent disciple {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu|1=Shinbutsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6606696}} succeeded him as the second head priest of Senjuji, followed by {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenchi|1=Kenchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6605658}}.
=== Sengoku Period === During the 15th century, the sect expanded significantly under the leadership of the 10th head priest, {{ill|Shin'e|ja|真恵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin'e|1=Shin'e|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q85271929}}, who actively spread Jōdo Shinshū teachings across {{ill|Tōkai region|en|Tōkai region|WD=Q398989|lt=Tōkai region}} and {{ill|Hokuriku region|en|Hokuriku region|WD=Q381025|lt=Hokuriku region}}. However, conflicts arose with {{ill|Rennyo|ja|蓮妃|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rennyo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the influential head of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honganji|1=Hongan-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11521048}}, leading many followers and temples to shift allegiance to the Honganji faction. In {{ill|1465|en|1465|WD=Q6556|lt=1465}}, Shin'e petitioned {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比士山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Hiei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to recognize Takada-ha as separate from Honganji, which was granted.
Despite setbacks due to military conflicts, including the {{ill|Echizen Ikkō-Ikki|ja|越前一向一挑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echizen Ikkō-Ikki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Takada-ha retained a strong presence in certain regions. By the 16th century, the sect moved its primary temple from {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|en|Shimotsuke Province|WD=Q1053699|lt=Shimotsuke Province}} to {{ill|Isshinden|ja|一身田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isshinden|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Ise Province|en|Ise Province|WD=Q864166|lt=Ise Province}}, where it remains today.
=== Edo Period === During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, Takada-ha maintained its status as one of the largest Jōdo Shinshū sects after the split of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honganji|1=Hongan-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11521048}} into the {{ill|Nishi Hongan-ji|en|Nishi Hongan-ji|WD=Q1146038|lt=Nishi Honganji}} and {{ill|Higashi Hongan-ji|en|Higashi Hongan-ji|WD=Q910281|lt=Higashi Honganji}} branches. The sect continued to flourish, with its head temple, Senjuji, recognized as a ''jun-monzeki'' (quasi-imperial temple).
== Major Temples == ; Head Temple * {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Honji Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}} (Mooka, Tochigi Prefecture)
; Main Temple * {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Honji Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}} (Isshinden, Tsu, Mie Prefecture)
; Branch Temples * Kyoto Betsuin * {{ill|Nagoya Betsuin|ja|專修寺名古屋別院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagoya Betsuin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * Kobe Betsuin * Hokkaido Betsuin (Kiyota-ku, Sapporo, Hokkaido) * Fukui Betsuin * Kanto Betsuin * Yokohama Betsuin
== Characteristics == Takada-ha uniquely uses ''Bunrui-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of the Pure Land|ja|文類慰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hymns of the Pure Land|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) for evening services, unlike other Jōdo Shinshū sects that primarily recite ''Shōshin-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of True Faith|ja|正信慰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hymns of True Faith|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). Additionally, its temples traditionally do not use {{ill|Tannishō|ja|商異止|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tannishō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, as the sect preserves a large collection of Shinran's original writings.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:真宗高田派]][[zh:真宗高田派]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:真宗高田派]][[zh:真宗高田派]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:真宗高田派]][[zh:真宗高田派]]
{{Infobox organization | name = Shinshū Takada-ha | native_name = 真宗高田派 | image = [[File:Shinshu Takada-ha Shumuin.JPG|250px]] | caption = The Religious Affairs Office at the head temple [[:en:Senjuji|Senjuji]] | type = [[Religious corporation]] | headquarters = 2819 Isshinden-chō, Tsu City, Mie Prefecture | leader_title = Hōshu (Head Priest) | leader_name = Jishō | website = {{URL|http://www.senjuji.or.jp/}} }}
'''Shinshū Takada-ha''' (真宗高田派) is a branch of {{ill|Jōdo Shinshū|ja|淡土真宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōdo Shinshū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, with its head temple at {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Honji Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}} in {{ill|Tsu, Mie|ja|渡哥市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsu, Mie|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It traces its origins to the disciples {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu|1=Shinbutsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6606696}} and {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenchi|1=Kenchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6605658}}, who led the Highada-monto community in the medieval period. The sect has approximately 640 affiliated temples.
== History ==
=== Founding Period === In {{ill|1205|en|1205|WD=Q5229|lt=1205}}, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|en|Emperor Go-Toba|WD=Q316658|lt=Emperor Go-Toba}}, the practice of {{ill|Senju Nembutsu|ja|専修念仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senju Nembutsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 称名念仏|1=chengming nianfo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11596185}}, propagated by {{ill|Hōnen|en|Hōnen|WD=Q507980|lt=Hōnen}} and his disciples, was banned, leading to the exile of {{ill|Shinran|ja|親熊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinran|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to {{ill|Echigo Province|en|Echigo Province|WD=Q666514|lt=Echigo Province}}. After being pardoned in {{ill|1211|en|1211|WD=Q5251|lt=1211}}, Shinran traveled to {{ill|Zenkō-ji|en|Zenkō-ji|WD=Q189640|lt=Zenkoji}} and then settled in {{ill|Hitachi Province|ja|常陽国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitachi Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where he began his missionary activities.
Shinran established his base at {{ill|Nishi Nembutsuji|ja|西念仏寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishi Nembutsuji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Hitachi, and in {{ill|1224|en|1224|WD=Q5402|lt=1224}}, he completed his seminal work, {{ill|Kyōgyōshinshō|ja|顯淡土真実教行証文類|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōgyōshinshō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ('Teaching, Practice, Faith, and Attainment'), which laid the doctrinal foundation for Jōdo Shinshū.
In {{ill|1225|en|1225|WD=Q5403|lt=1225}}, according to tradition, Shinran received a revelation instructing him to establish a temple in Highada. With the support of {{ill|Ōuchi clan|en|Ōuchi clan|WD=Q1348909|lt=Ōuchi Clan}}, the ruler of {{ill|Mooka Castle|ja|真岡城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mooka Castle|1=Mooka Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11583106}}, he enshrined a replica of the {{ill|Zenkoji-style Amida Triad|ja|善光寺式阿弥陀三宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenkoji-style Amida Triad|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in what became {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Honji Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}}, marking the origins of the Highada-monto tradition and the Shinshū Takada-ha sect.
The temple was officially named ''Senshū Amida-ji'' in {{ill|1226|en|1226|WD=Q5405|lt=1226}} by imperial decree, and Shinran spent about seven years there before returning to Kyoto. After Shinran's departure, his prominent disciple {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu|1=Shinbutsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6606696}} succeeded him as the second head priest of Senjuji, followed by {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenchi|1=Kenchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6605658}}.
=== Sengoku Period === During the 15th century, the sect expanded significantly under the leadership of the 10th head priest, {{ill|Shin'e|ja|真恵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin'e|1=Shin'e|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q85271929}}, who actively spread Jōdo Shinshū teachings across {{ill|Tōkai region|en|Tōkai region|WD=Q398989|lt=Tōkai region}} and {{ill|Hokuriku region|en|Hokuriku region|WD=Q381025|lt=Hokuriku region}}. However, conflicts arose with {{ill|Rennyo|ja|蓮妃|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rennyo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the influential head of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honganji|1=Hongan-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11521048}}, leading many followers and temples to shift allegiance to the Honganji faction. In {{ill|1465|en|1465|WD=Q6556|lt=1465}}, Shin'e petitioned {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比士山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Hiei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to recognize Takada-ha as separate from Honganji, which was granted.
Despite setbacks due to military conflicts, including the {{ill|Echizen Ikkō-Ikki|ja|越前一向一挑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echizen Ikkō-Ikki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Takada-ha retained a strong presence in certain regions. By the 16th century, the sect moved its primary temple from {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|en|Shimotsuke Province|WD=Q1053699|lt=Shimotsuke Province}} to {{ill|Isshinden|ja|一身田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isshinden|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Ise Province|en|Ise Province|WD=Q864166|lt=Ise Province}}, where it remains today.
=== Edo Period === During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, Takada-ha maintained its status as one of the largest Jōdo Shinshū sects after the split of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honganji|1=Hongan-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11521048}} into the {{ill|Nishi Hongan-ji|en|Nishi Hongan-ji|WD=Q1146038|lt=Nishi Honganji}} and {{ill|Higashi Hongan-ji|en|Higashi Hongan-ji|WD=Q910281|lt=Higashi Honganji}} branches. The sect continued to flourish, with its head temple, Senjuji, recognized as a ''jun-monzeki'' (quasi-imperial temple).
== Major Temples == ; Head Temple * {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Honji Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}} (Mooka, Tochigi Prefecture)
; Main Temple * {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Honji Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}} (Isshinden, Tsu, Mie Prefecture)
; Branch Temples * Kyoto Betsuin * {{ill|Nagoya Betsuin|ja|專修寺名古屋別院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagoya Betsuin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * Kobe Betsuin * Hokkaido Betsuin (Kiyota-ku, Sapporo, Hokkaido) * Fukui Betsuin * Kanto Betsuin * Yokohama Betsuin
== Characteristics == Takada-ha uniquely uses ''Bunrui-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of the Pure Land|ja|文類慰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hymns of the Pure Land|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) for evening services, unlike other Jōdo Shinshū sects that primarily recite ''Shōshin-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of True Faith|ja|正信慰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hymns of True Faith|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). Additionally, its temples traditionally do not use {{ill|Tannishō|ja|商異止|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tannishō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, as the sect preserves a large collection of Shinran's original writings.
== External Links == * [http://www.honji-senjuji.jp/ Honji Senjuji] * [http://www.senjuji.or.jp/ Senjuji] ) * {{ill|Kimpusen-ji|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kimpusen-ji|qq=}} (Yoshino) * {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōfuku-ji|qq=}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:真宗高田派]][[zh:真宗高田派]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:真宗高田派]][[zh:真宗高田派]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:真宗高田派]][[zh:真宗高田派]]
{{Infobox organization | name = Shinshū Takada-ha | native_name = 真宗高田派 | image = [[File:Shinshu Takada-ha Shumuin.JPG|250px]] | caption = The Religious Affairs Office at the head temple [[:en:Senjuji|Senjuji]] | type = [[Religious corporation]] | headquarters = 2819 Isshinden-chō, Tsu City, Mie Prefecture | leader_title = Hōshu (Head Priest) | leader_name = Jishō | website = {{URL|http://www.senjuji.or.jp/}} }}
'''Shinshū Takada-ha''' (真宗高田派) is a branch of {{ill|Jōdo Shinshū|ja|淡土真宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōdo Shinshū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, with its head temple at {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Honji Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}} in {{ill|Tsu, Mie|ja|渡哥市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsu, Mie|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It traces its origins to the disciples {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu|1=Shinbutsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6606696}} and {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenchi|1=Kenchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6605658}}, who led the Highada-monto community in the medieval period. The sect has approximately 640 affiliated temples.
== History ==
=== Founding Period === In {{ill|1205|en|1205|WD=Q5229|lt=1205}}, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|en|Emperor Go-Toba|WD=Q316658|lt=Emperor Go-Toba}}, the practice of {{ill|Senju Nembutsu|ja|専修念仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senju Nembutsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 称名念仏|1=chengming nianfo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11596185}}, propagated by {{ill|Hōnen|en|Hōnen|WD=Q507980|lt=Hōnen}} and his disciples, was banned, leading to the exile of {{ill|Shinran|ja|親熊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinran|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to {{ill|Echigo Province|en|Echigo Province|WD=Q666514|lt=Echigo Province}}. After being pardoned in {{ill|1211|en|1211|WD=Q5251|lt=1211}}, Shinran traveled to {{ill|Zenkō-ji|en|Zenkō-ji|WD=Q189640|lt=Zenkoji}} and then settled in {{ill|Hitachi Province|ja|常陽国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitachi Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where he began his missionary activities.
Shinran established his base at {{ill|Nishi Nembutsuji|ja|西念仏寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishi Nembutsuji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Hitachi, and in {{ill|1224|en|1224|WD=Q5402|lt=1224}}, he completed his seminal work, {{ill|Kyōgyōshinshō|ja|顯淡土真実教行証文類|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōgyōshinshō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ('Teaching, Practice, Faith, and Attainment'), which laid the doctrinal foundation for Jōdo Shinshū.
In {{ill|1225|en|1225|WD=Q5403|lt=1225}}, according to tradition, Shinran received a revelation instructing him to establish a temple in Highada. With the support of {{ill|Ōuchi clan|en|Ōuchi clan|WD=Q1348909|lt=Ōuchi Clan}}, the ruler of {{ill|Mooka Castle|ja|真岡城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mooka Castle|1=Mooka Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11583106}}, he enshrined a replica of the {{ill|Zenkoji-style Amida Triad|ja|善光寺式阿弥陀三宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenkoji-style Amida Triad|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in what became {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Honji Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}}, marking the origins of the Highada-monto tradition and the Shinshū Takada-ha sect.
The temple was officially named ''Senshū Amida-ji'' in {{ill|1226|en|1226|WD=Q5405|lt=1226}} by imperial decree, and Shinran spent about seven years there before returning to Kyoto. After Shinran's departure, his prominent disciple {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu|1=Shinbutsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6606696}} succeeded him as the second head priest of Senjuji, followed by {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenchi|1=Kenchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6605658}}.
=== Sengoku Period === During the 15th century, the sect expanded significantly under the leadership of the 10th head priest, {{ill|Shin'e|ja|真恵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin'e|1=Shin'e|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q85271929}}, who actively spread Jōdo Shinshū teachings across {{ill|Tōkai region|en|Tōkai region|WD=Q398989|lt=Tōkai region}} and {{ill|Hokuriku region|en|Hokuriku region|WD=Q381025|lt=Hokuriku region}}. However, conflicts arose with {{ill|Rennyo|ja|蓮妃|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rennyo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the influential head of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honganji|1=Hongan-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11521048}}, leading many followers and temples to shift allegiance to the Honganji faction. In {{ill|1465|en|1465|WD=Q6556|lt=1465}}, Shin'e petitioned {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比士山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Hiei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to recognize Takada-ha as separate from Honganji, which was granted.
Despite setbacks due to military conflicts, including the {{ill|Echizen Ikkō-Ikki|ja|越前一向一挑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echizen Ikkō-Ikki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Takada-ha retained a strong presence in certain regions. By the 16th century, the sect moved its primary temple from {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|en|Shimotsuke Province|WD=Q1053699|lt=Shimotsuke Province}} to {{ill|Isshinden|ja|一身田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isshinden|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Ise Province|en|Ise Province|WD=Q864166|lt=Ise Province}}, where it remains today.
=== Edo Period === During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, Takada-ha maintained its status as one of the largest Jōdo Shinshū sects after the split of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honganji|1=Hongan-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11521048}} into the {{ill|Nishi Hongan-ji|en|Nishi Hongan-ji|WD=Q1146038|lt=Nishi Honganji}} and {{ill|Higashi Hongan-ji|en|Higashi Hongan-ji|WD=Q910281|lt=Higashi Honganji}} branches. The sect continued to flourish, with its head temple, Senjuji, recognized as a ''jun-monzeki'' (quasi-imperial temple).
== Major Temples == ; Head Temple * {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Honji Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}} (Mooka, Tochigi Prefecture)
; Main Temple * {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Honji Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}} (Isshinden, Tsu, Mie Prefecture)
; Branch Temples * Kyoto Betsuin * {{ill|Nagoya Betsuin|ja|專修寺名古屋別院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagoya Betsuin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * Kobe Betsuin * Hokkaido Betsuin (Kiyota-ku, Sapporo, Hokkaido) * Fukui Betsuin * Kanto Betsuin * Yokohama Betsuin
== Characteristics == Takada-ha uniquely uses ''Bunrui-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of the Pure Land|ja|文類慰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hymns of the Pure Land|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) for evening services, unlike other Jōdo Shinshū sects that primarily recite ''Shōshin-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of True Faith|ja|正信慰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hymns of True Faith|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). Additionally, its temples traditionally do not use {{ill|Tannishō|ja|商異止|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tannishō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, as the sect preserves a large collection of Shinran's original writings.

== References == <references />

{{Infobox organization | name = Shinshū Takada-ha | native_name = 真宗高田派 | image = [[File:Shinshu Takada-ha Shumuin.JPG|250px]] | caption = The Religious Affairs Office at the head temple [[:en:Senjuji|Senjuji]] | type = [[Religious corporation]] | headquarters = 2819 Isshinden-chō, Tsu City, Mie Prefecture | leader_title = Hōshu (Head Priest) | leader_name = Jishō | website = {{URL|http://www.senjuji.or.jp/}} }}
'''Shinshū Takada-ha''' (真宗高田派) is a branch of {{ill|Jōdo Shinshū|ja|淡土真宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōdo Shinshū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, with its head temple at {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}} in {{ill|Tsu, Mie|ja|渡哥市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsu, Mie|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It traces its origins to the disciples {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu|1=Shinbutsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6606696}} and {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenchi|1=Kenchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6605658}}, who led the Highada-monto community in the medieval period. The sect has approximately 640 affiliated temples.
== History ==
=== Founding Period === In {{ill|1205|en|1205|WD=Q5229|lt=1205|1=1205}}, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|en|Emperor Go-Toba|WD=Q316658|lt=Emperor Go-Toba|1=Emperor Go-Toba}}, the practice of {{ill|Senju Nembutsu|ja|専修念仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senju Nembutsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 称名念仏|1=chengming nianfo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11596185}}, propagated by {{ill|Hōnen|en|Hōnen|WD=Q507980|lt=Hōnen|1=Hōnen}} and his disciples, was banned, leading to the exile of {{ill|Shinran|ja|親熊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinran|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to {{ill|Echigo Province|en|Echigo Province|WD=Q666514|lt=Echigo Province|1=Echigo Province}}. After being pardoned in {{ill|1211|en|1211|WD=Q5251|lt=1211|1=1211}}, Shinran traveled to {{ill|Zenkō-ji|en|Zenkō-ji|WD=Q189640|lt=Zenkoji|1=Zenkō-ji}} and then settled in {{ill|Hitachi Province|ja|常陽国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitachi Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where he began his missionary activities.
Shinran established his base at {{ill|Nishi Nembutsuji|ja|西念仏寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishi Nembutsuji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Hitachi, and in {{ill|1224|en|1224|WD=Q5402|lt=1224|1=1224}}, he completed his seminal work, {{ill|Kyōgyōshinshō|ja|顯淡土真実教行証文類|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōgyōshinshō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ('Teaching, Practice, Faith, and Attainment'), which laid the doctrinal foundation for Jōdo Shinshū.
In {{ill|1225|en|1225|WD=Q5403|lt=1225|1=1225}}, according to tradition, Shinran received a revelation instructing him to establish a temple in Highada. With the support of {{ill|Ōuchi clan|en|Ōuchi clan|WD=Q1348909|lt=Ōuchi Clan|1=Ōuchi clan}}, the ruler of {{ill|Mooka Castle|ja|真岡城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mooka Castle|1=Q11583106|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11583106}}, he enshrined a replica of the {{ill|Zenkoji-style Amida Triad|ja|善光寺式阿弥陀三宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenkoji-style Amida Triad|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in what became {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}}, marking the origins of the Highada-monto tradition and the Shinshū Takada-ha sect.
The temple was officially named ''Senshū Amida-ji'' in {{ill|1226|en|1226|WD=Q5405|lt=1226|1=1226}} by imperial decree, and Shinran spent about seven years there before returning to Kyoto. After Shinran's departure, his prominent disciple {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu|1=Shinbutsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6606696}} succeeded him as the second head priest of Senjuji, followed by {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenchi|1=Kenchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6605658}}.
=== Sengoku Period === During the 15th century, the sect expanded significantly under the leadership of the 10th head priest, {{ill|Shin'e|ja|真恵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin'e|1=Q85271929|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q85271929}}, who actively spread Jōdo Shinshū teachings across {{ill|Tōkai region|en|Tōkai region|WD=Q398989|lt=Tōkai region|1=Tōkai region}} and {{ill|Hokuriku region|en|Hokuriku region|WD=Q381025|lt=Hokuriku region|1=Hokuriku region}}. However, conflicts arose with {{ill|Rennyo|ja|蓮妃|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rennyo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the influential head of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honganji|1=Hongan-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11521048}}, leading many followers and temples to shift allegiance to the Honganji faction. In {{ill|1465|en|1465|WD=Q6556|lt=1465|1=1465}}, Shin'e petitioned {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比士山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Hiei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to recognize Takada-ha as separate from Honganji, which was granted.
Despite setbacks due to military conflicts, including the {{ill|Echizen Ikkō-Ikki|ja|越前一向一挑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echizen Ikkō-Ikki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Takada-ha retained a strong presence in certain regions. By the 16th century, the sect moved its primary temple from {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|en|Shimotsuke Province|WD=Q1053699|lt=Shimotsuke Province|1=Shimotsuke Province}} to {{ill|Isshinden|ja|一身田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isshinden|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Ise Province|en|Ise Province|WD=Q864166|lt=Ise Province|1=Ise Province}}, where it remains today.
=== Edo Period === During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, Takada-ha maintained its status as one of the largest Jōdo Shinshū sects after the split of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honganji|1=Hongan-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11521048}} into the {{ill|Nishi Hongan-ji|en|Nishi Hongan-ji|WD=Q1146038|lt=Nishi Honganji|1=Nishi Hongan-ji}} and {{ill|Higashi Hongan-ji|en|Higashi Hongan-ji|WD=Q910281|lt=Higashi Honganji|1=Higashi Hongan-ji}} branches. The sect continued to flourish, with its head temple, Senjuji, recognized as a ''jun-monzeki'' (quasi-imperial temple).
== Major Temples == ; Head Temple * {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}} (Mooka, Tochigi Prefecture)
; Main Temple * {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}} (Isshinden, Tsu, Mie Prefecture)
; Branch Temples * Kyoto Betsuin * {{ill|Nagoya Betsuin|ja|專修寺名古屋別院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagoya Betsuin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * Kobe Betsuin * Hokkaido Betsuin (Kiyota-ku, Sapporo, Hokkaido) * Fukui Betsuin * Kanto Betsuin * Yokohama Betsuin
== Characteristics == Takada-ha uniquely uses ''Bunrui-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of the Pure Land|ja|文類慰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hymns of the Pure Land|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) for evening services, unlike other Jōdo Shinshū sects that primarily recite ''Shōshin-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of True Faith|ja|正信慰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hymns of True Faith|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). Additionally, its temples traditionally do not use {{ill|Tannishō|ja|商異止|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tannishō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, as the sect preserves a large collection of Shinran's original writings.

{{Infobox organization | name = Shinshū Takada-ha | native_name = 真宗高田派 | image = [[File:Shinshu Takada-ha Shumuin.JPG|250px]] | caption = The Religious Affairs Office at the head temple [[:en:Senjuji|Senjuji]] | type = [[Religious corporation]] | headquarters = 2819 Isshinden-chō, Tsu City, Mie Prefecture | leader_title = Hōshu (Head Priest) | leader_name = Jishō | website = {{URL|http://www.senjuji.or.jp/}} }}
'''Shinshū Takada-ha''' (真宗高田派) is a branch of {{ill|Jōdo Shinshū|ja|淡土真宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōdo Shinshū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, with its head temple at {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}} in {{ill|Tsu, Mie|ja|渡哥市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsu, Mie|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It traces its origins to the disciples {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu|1=Shinbutsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6606696}} and {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenchi|1=Kenchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6605658}}, who led the Highada-monto community in the medieval period. The sect has approximately 640 affiliated temples.
== History ==
=== Founding Period === In {{ill|1205|en|1205|WD=Q5229|lt=1205|1=1205}}, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|en|Emperor Go-Toba|WD=Q316658|lt=Emperor Go-Toba|1=Emperor Go-Toba}}, the practice of {{ill|Senju Nembutsu|ja|専修念仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senju Nembutsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 称名念仏|1=chengming nianfo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11596185}}, propagated by {{ill|Hōnen|en|Hōnen|WD=Q507980|lt=Hōnen|1=Hōnen}} and his disciples, was banned, leading to the exile of {{ill|Shinran|ja|親熊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinran|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to {{ill|Echigo Province|en|Echigo Province|WD=Q666514|lt=Echigo Province|1=Echigo Province}}. After being pardoned in {{ill|1211|en|1211|WD=Q5251|lt=1211|1=1211}}, Shinran traveled to {{ill|Zenkō-ji|en|Zenkō-ji|WD=Q189640|lt=Zenkoji|1=Zenkō-ji}} and then settled in {{ill|Hitachi Province|ja|常陽国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitachi Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where he began his missionary activities.
Shinran established his base at {{ill|Nishi Nembutsuji|ja|西念仏寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishi Nembutsuji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Hitachi, and in {{ill|1224|en|1224|WD=Q5402|lt=1224|1=1224}}, he completed his seminal work, {{ill|Kyōgyōshinshō|ja|顯淡土真実教行証文類|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōgyōshinshō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ('Teaching, Practice, Faith, and Attainment'), which laid the doctrinal foundation for Jōdo Shinshū.
In {{ill|1225|en|1225|WD=Q5403|lt=1225|1=1225}}, according to tradition, Shinran received a revelation instructing him to establish a temple in Highada. With the support of {{ill|Ōuchi clan|en|Ōuchi clan|WD=Q1348909|lt=Ōuchi Clan|1=Ōuchi clan}}, the ruler of {{ill|Mooka Castle|ja|真岡城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mooka Castle|1=Q11583106|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11583106}}, he enshrined a replica of the {{ill|Zenkoji-style Amida Triad|ja|善光寺式阿弥陀三宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenkoji-style Amida Triad|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in what became {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}}, marking the origins of the Highada-monto tradition and the Shinshū Takada-ha sect.
The temple was officially named ''Senshū Amida-ji'' in {{ill|1226|en|1226|WD=Q5405|lt=1226|1=1226}} by imperial decree, and Shinran spent about seven years there before returning to Kyoto. After Shinran's departure, his prominent disciple {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu|1=Shinbutsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6606696}} succeeded him as the second head priest of Senjuji, followed by {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenchi|1=Kenchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6605658}}.
=== Sengoku Period === During the 15th century, the sect expanded significantly under the leadership of the 10th head priest, {{ill|Shin'e|ja|真恵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin'e|1=Q85271929|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q85271929}}, who actively spread Jōdo Shinshū teachings across {{ill|Tōkai region|en|Tōkai region|WD=Q398989|lt=Tōkai region|1=Tōkai region}} and {{ill|Hokuriku region|en|Hokuriku region|WD=Q381025|lt=Hokuriku region|1=Hokuriku region}}. However, conflicts arose with {{ill|Rennyo|ja|蓮妃|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rennyo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the influential head of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honganji|1=Hongan-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11521048}}, leading many followers and temples to shift allegiance to the Honganji faction. In {{ill|1465|en|1465|WD=Q6556|lt=1465|1=1465}}, Shin'e petitioned {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比士山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Hiei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to recognize Takada-ha as separate from Honganji, which was granted.
Despite setbacks due to military conflicts, including the {{ill|Echizen Ikkō-Ikki|ja|越前一向一挑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echizen Ikkō-Ikki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Takada-ha retained a strong presence in certain regions. By the 16th century, the sect moved its primary temple from {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|en|Shimotsuke Province|WD=Q1053699|lt=Shimotsuke Province|1=Shimotsuke Province}} to {{ill|Isshinden|ja|一身田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isshinden|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Ise Province|en|Ise Province|WD=Q864166|lt=Ise Province|1=Ise Province}}, where it remains today.
=== Edo Period === During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, Takada-ha maintained its status as one of the largest Jōdo Shinshū sects after the split of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honganji|1=Hongan-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11521048}} into the {{ill|Nishi Hongan-ji|en|Nishi Hongan-ji|WD=Q1146038|lt=Nishi Honganji|1=Nishi Hongan-ji}} and {{ill|Higashi Hongan-ji|en|Higashi Hongan-ji|WD=Q910281|lt=Higashi Honganji|1=Higashi Hongan-ji}} branches. The sect continued to flourish, with its head temple, Senjuji, recognized as a ''jun-monzeki'' (quasi-imperial temple).

== Characteristics == Takada-ha uniquely uses ''Bunrui-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of the Pure Land|ja|文類慰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hymns of the Pure Land|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) for evening services, unlike other Jōdo Shinshū sects that primarily recite ''Shōshin-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of True Faith|ja|正信慰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hymns of True Faith|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). Additionally, its temples traditionally do not use {{ill|Tannishō|ja|商異止|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tannishō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, as the sect preserves a large collection of Shinran's original writings.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:真宗高田派]][[zh:真宗高田派]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:真宗高田派]][[zh:真宗高田派]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:真宗高田派]][[zh:真宗高田派]]
{{Infobox organization | name = Shinshū Takada-ha | native_name = 真宗高田派 | image = [[File:Shinshu Takada-ha Shumuin.JPG|250px]] | caption = The Religious Affairs Office at the head temple [[:en:Senjuji|Senjuji]] | type = [[Religious corporation]] | headquarters = 2819 Isshinden-chō, Tsu City, Mie Prefecture | leader_title = Hōshu (Head Priest) | leader_name = Jishō | website = {{URL|http://www.senjuji.or.jp/}} }}
'''Shinshū Takada-ha''' (真宗高田派) is a branch of {{ill|Jōdo Shinshū|ja|淡土真宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōdo Shinshū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, with its head temple at {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}} in {{ill|Tsu, Mie|ja|渡哥市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsu, Mie|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It traces its origins to the disciples {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu|1=Shinbutsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6606696}} and {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenchi|1=Kenchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6605658}}, who led the Highada-monto community in the medieval period. The sect has approximately 640 affiliated temples.
== History ==
=== Founding Period === In {{ill|1205|en|1205|WD=Q5229|lt=1205|1=1205}}, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|en|Emperor Go-Toba|WD=Q316658|lt=Emperor Go-Toba|1=Emperor Go-Toba}}, the practice of {{ill|Senju Nembutsu|ja|専修念仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senju Nembutsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 称名念仏|1=chengming nianfo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11596185}}, propagated by {{ill|Hōnen|en|Hōnen|WD=Q507980|lt=Hōnen|1=Hōnen}} and his disciples, was banned, leading to the exile of {{ill|Shinran|ja|親熊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinran|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to {{ill|Echigo Province|en|Echigo Province|WD=Q666514|lt=Echigo Province|1=Echigo Province}}. After being pardoned in {{ill|1211|en|1211|WD=Q5251|lt=1211|1=1211}}, Shinran traveled to {{ill|Zenkō-ji|en|Zenkō-ji|WD=Q189640|lt=Zenkoji|1=Zenkō-ji}} and then settled in {{ill|Hitachi Province|ja|常陽国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitachi Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where he began his missionary activities.
Shinran established his base at {{ill|Nishi Nembutsuji|ja|西念仏寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishi Nembutsuji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Hitachi, and in {{ill|1224|en|1224|WD=Q5402|lt=1224|1=1224}}, he completed his seminal work, {{ill|Kyōgyōshinshō|ja|顯淡土真実教行証文類|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōgyōshinshō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ('Teaching, Practice, Faith, and Attainment'), which laid the doctrinal foundation for Jōdo Shinshū.
In {{ill|1225|en|1225|WD=Q5403|lt=1225|1=1225}}, according to tradition, Shinran received a revelation instructing him to establish a temple in Highada. With the support of {{ill|Ōuchi clan|en|Ōuchi clan|WD=Q1348909|lt=Ōuchi Clan|1=Ōuchi clan}}, the ruler of {{ill|Mooka Castle|ja|真岡城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mooka Castle|1=Q11583106|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11583106}}, he enshrined a replica of the {{ill|Zenkoji-style Amida Triad|ja|善光寺式阿弥陀三宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenkoji-style Amida Triad|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in what became {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}}, marking the origins of the Highada-monto tradition and the Shinshū Takada-ha sect.
The temple was officially named ''Senshū Amida-ji'' in {{ill|1226|en|1226|WD=Q5405|lt=1226|1=1226}} by imperial decree, and Shinran spent about seven years there before returning to Kyoto. After Shinran's departure, his prominent disciple {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu|1=Shinbutsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6606696}} succeeded him as the second head priest of Senjuji, followed by {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenchi|1=Kenchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6605658}}.
=== Sengoku Period === During the 15th century, the sect expanded significantly under the leadership of the 10th head priest, {{ill|Shin'e|ja|真恵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin'e|1=Q85271929|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q85271929}}, who actively spread Jōdo Shinshū teachings across {{ill|Tōkai region|en|Tōkai region|WD=Q398989|lt=Tōkai region|1=Tōkai region}} and {{ill|Hokuriku region|en|Hokuriku region|WD=Q381025|lt=Hokuriku region|1=Hokuriku region}}. However, conflicts arose with {{ill|Rennyo|ja|蓮妃|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rennyo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the influential head of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honganji|1=Hongan-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11521048}}, leading many followers and temples to shift allegiance to the Honganji faction. In {{ill|1465|en|1465|WD=Q6556|lt=1465|1=1465}}, Shin'e petitioned {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比士山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Hiei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to recognize Takada-ha as separate from Honganji, which was granted.
Despite setbacks due to military conflicts, including the {{ill|Echizen Ikkō-Ikki|ja|越前一向一挑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echizen Ikkō-Ikki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Takada-ha retained a strong presence in certain regions. By the 16th century, the sect moved its primary temple from {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|en|Shimotsuke Province|WD=Q1053699|lt=Shimotsuke Province|1=Shimotsuke Province}} to {{ill|Isshinden|ja|一身田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isshinden|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Ise Province|en|Ise Province|WD=Q864166|lt=Ise Province|1=Ise Province}}, where it remains today.
=== Edo Period === During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, Takada-ha maintained its status as one of the largest Jōdo Shinshū sects after the split of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honganji|1=Hongan-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11521048}} into the {{ill|Nishi Hongan-ji|en|Nishi Hongan-ji|WD=Q1146038|lt=Nishi Honganji|1=Nishi Hongan-ji}} and {{ill|Higashi Hongan-ji|en|Higashi Hongan-ji|WD=Q910281|lt=Higashi Honganji|1=Higashi Hongan-ji}} branches. The sect continued to flourish, with its head temple, Senjuji, recognized as a ''jun-monzeki'' (quasi-imperial temple).

== Characteristics == Takada-ha uniquely uses ''Bunrui-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of the Pure Land|ja|文類慰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hymns of the Pure Land|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) for evening services, unlike other Jōdo Shinshū sects that primarily recite ''Shōshin-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of True Faith|ja|正信慰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hymns of True Faith|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). Additionally, its temples traditionally do not use {{ill|Tannishō|ja|商異止|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tannishō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, as the sect preserves a large collection of Shinran's original writings.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:真宗高田派]][[zh:真宗高田派]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:真宗高田派]][[zh:真宗高田派]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:真宗高田派]][[zh:真宗高田派]]
{{Infobox organization | name = Shinshū Takada-ha | native_name = 真宗高田派 | image = [[File:Shinshu Takada-ha Shumuin.JPG|250px]] | caption = The Religious Affairs Office at the head temple [[:en:Senjuji|Senjuji]] | type = [[Religious corporation]] | headquarters = 2819 Isshinden-chō, Tsu City, Mie Prefecture | leader_title = Hōshu (Head Priest) | leader_name = Jishō | website = {{URL|http://www.senjuji.or.jp/}} }}
'''Shinshū Takada-ha''' (真宗高田派) is a branch of {{ill|Jōdo Shinshū|ja|淡土真宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōdo Shinshū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, with its head temple at {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}} in {{ill|Tsu, Mie|ja|渡哥市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsu, Mie|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It traces its origins to the disciples {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu|1=Shinbutsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6606696}} and {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenchi|1=Kenchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6605658}}, who led the Highada-monto community in the medieval period. The sect has approximately 640 affiliated temples.
== History ==
=== Founding Period === In {{ill|1205|en|1205|WD=Q5229|lt=1205|1=1205}}, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|en|Emperor Go-Toba|WD=Q316658|lt=Emperor Go-Toba|1=Emperor Go-Toba}}, the practice of {{ill|Senju Nembutsu|ja|専修念仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senju Nembutsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 称名念仏|1=chengming nianfo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11596185}}, propagated by {{ill|Hōnen|en|Hōnen|WD=Q507980|lt=Hōnen|1=Hōnen}} and his disciples, was banned, leading to the exile of {{ill|Shinran|ja|親熊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinran|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to {{ill|Echigo Province|en|Echigo Province|WD=Q666514|lt=Echigo Province|1=Echigo Province}}. After being pardoned in {{ill|1211|en|1211|WD=Q5251|lt=1211|1=1211}}, Shinran traveled to {{ill|Zenkō-ji|en|Zenkō-ji|WD=Q189640|lt=Zenkoji|1=Zenkō-ji}} and then settled in {{ill|Hitachi Province|ja|常陽国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitachi Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where he began his missionary activities.
Shinran established his base at {{ill|Nishi Nembutsuji|ja|西念仏寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishi Nembutsuji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Hitachi, and in {{ill|1224|en|1224|WD=Q5402|lt=1224|1=1224}}, he completed his seminal work, {{ill|Kyōgyōshinshō|ja|顯淡土真実教行証文類|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōgyōshinshō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ('Teaching, Practice, Faith, and Attainment'), which laid the doctrinal foundation for Jōdo Shinshū.
In {{ill|1225|en|1225|WD=Q5403|lt=1225|1=1225}}, according to tradition, Shinran received a revelation instructing him to establish a temple in Highada. With the support of {{ill|Ōuchi clan|en|Ōuchi clan|WD=Q1348909|lt=Ōuchi Clan|1=Ōuchi clan}}, the ruler of {{ill|Mooka Castle|ja|真岡城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mooka Castle|1=Q11583106|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11583106}}, he enshrined a replica of the {{ill|Zenkoji-style Amida Triad|ja|善光寺式阿弥陀三宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenkoji-style Amida Triad|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in what became {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}}, marking the origins of the Highada-monto tradition and the Shinshū Takada-ha sect.
The temple was officially named ''Senshū Amida-ji'' in {{ill|1226|en|1226|WD=Q5405|lt=1226|1=1226}} by imperial decree, and Shinran spent about seven years there before returning to Kyoto. After Shinran's departure, his prominent disciple {{ill|Shinbutsu|ja|真仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu|1=Shinbutsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6606696}} succeeded him as the second head priest of Senjuji, followed by {{ill|Kenchi|ja|顕智|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenchi|1=Kenchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6605658}}.
=== Sengoku Period === During the 15th century, the sect expanded significantly under the leadership of the 10th head priest, {{ill|Shin'e|ja|真恵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin'e|1=Q85271929|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q85271929}}, who actively spread Jōdo Shinshū teachings across {{ill|Tōkai region|en|Tōkai region|WD=Q398989|lt=Tōkai region|1=Tōkai region}} and {{ill|Hokuriku region|en|Hokuriku region|WD=Q381025|lt=Hokuriku region|1=Hokuriku region}}. However, conflicts arose with {{ill|Rennyo|ja|蓮妃|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rennyo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the influential head of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honganji|1=Hongan-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11521048}}, leading many followers and temples to shift allegiance to the Honganji faction. In {{ill|1465|en|1465|WD=Q6556|lt=1465|1=1465}}, Shin'e petitioned {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比士山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Hiei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to recognize Takada-ha as separate from Honganji, which was granted.
Despite setbacks due to military conflicts, including the {{ill|Echizen Ikkō-Ikki|ja|越前一向一挑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echizen Ikkō-Ikki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Takada-ha retained a strong presence in certain regions. By the 16th century, the sect moved its primary temple from {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|en|Shimotsuke Province|WD=Q1053699|lt=Shimotsuke Province|1=Shimotsuke Province}} to {{ill|Isshinden|ja|一身田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isshinden|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Ise Province|en|Ise Province|WD=Q864166|lt=Ise Province|1=Ise Province}}, where it remains today.
=== Edo Period === During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, Takada-ha maintained its status as one of the largest Jōdo Shinshū sects after the split of {{ill|Honganji|ja|本願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honganji|1=Hongan-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11521048}} into the {{ill|Nishi Hongan-ji|en|Nishi Hongan-ji|WD=Q1146038|lt=Nishi Honganji|1=Nishi Hongan-ji}} and {{ill|Higashi Hongan-ji|en|Higashi Hongan-ji|WD=Q910281|lt=Higashi Honganji|1=Higashi Hongan-ji}} branches. The sect continued to flourish, with its head temple, Senjuji, recognized as a ''jun-monzeki'' (quasi-imperial temple).
== Major Temples == ; Head Temple * {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}} (Mooka, Tochigi Prefecture)
; Main Temple * {{ill|Senjuji|ja|專修寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjuji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 専修寺|1=Senju-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3478562}} (Isshinden, Tsu, Mie Prefecture)
; Branch Temples * Kyoto Betsuin * {{ill|Nagoya Betsuin|ja|專修寺名古屋別院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagoya Betsuin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * Kobe Betsuin * Hokkaido Betsuin (Kiyota-ku, Sapporo, Hokkaido) * Fukui Betsuin * Kanto Betsuin * Yokohama Betsuin
== Characteristics == Takada-ha uniquely uses ''Bunrui-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of the Pure Land|ja|文類慰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hymns of the Pure Land|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) for evening services, unlike other Jōdo Shinshū sects that primarily recite ''Shōshin-ge'' ({{ill|Hymns of True Faith|ja|正信慰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hymns of True Faith|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). Additionally, its temples traditionally do not use {{ill|Tannishō|ja|商異止|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tannishō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, as the sect preserves a large collection of Shinran's original writings.
